blind_framing: MONITORING ALERT — Kethara Wetland Station, Grid K-9. Biological productivity sensors in the eastern basin have recorded a significant anomaly over the past 8 weeks. Field verification confirmed: readings are real. Station director requests investigation to identify the cause of the collapse in fish assemblage productivity. You have access to the station data network.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Delvorn irrigation expansion increased phosphorus and nitrogen runoff into Kethara's eastern basin. This triggered a Vorex microorganism bloom. As water temperature shifted, Vorex declined and Pellucid/Strathin microorganisms replaced it (succession). This community shift disrupted dissolved oxygen and eliminated the algal food structure that Finnek fish depend on. Fish died from food-structure loss and oxygen disruption during succession — not from Vorex toxicity.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Bloom succession transition as mortality driver — the transition between algal communities (not the peak of either) is the moment of collapse. This concept is never named in the graph.

nodes.label: KETHARA_PRODUCTIVITY_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: ALERT — Kethara Wetland Eastern Basin (Grid K-9). Productivity sensors show an 81% decline in fish assemblage density over 8 weeks. Dissolved oxygen readings fluctuating. Temperature: within normal seasonal range. Field team confirms: readings are real, not equipment failure.
nodes.inspection_detail: The decline began in the shallow eastern margin and has spread inward. Neighboring western basin shows no anomaly. Spatial pattern is consistent with an influence entering from the eastern margin.

nodes.label: FINNEK_POPULATION_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Finnek Fish Census — Eastern Basin. Current count: 420 individuals. Eight-week baseline: 2,200. Decline: 81%. Finnek are mid-trophic filter feeders with a narrow dietary preference for Vorex-era algal food structures. Reproductive biology normal 6 months prior.
nodes.inspection_detail: Finnek require a specific algal food matrix — they do not adapt rapidly to new food structures. Their decline is consistent with either a toxin or a habitat/food disruption.

nodes.label: VOREX_TOXIN_HYPOTHESIS
nodes.surface_text: Vorex Toxin Assessment. Vorex microorganisms produce compound KX-4 at high densities. Station records document Vorex bloom 6 weeks ago reaching 3.1 million cells/mL. KX-4 lethal threshold for Finnek: 2.2 mg/L. Peak KX-4 concentration recorded during bloom: 0.8 mg/L — 36% of lethal threshold. DISCONFIRMATION: KX-4 never reached a lethal concentration during the Vorex bloom peak.
nodes.inspection_detail: Toxin profiles from weekly water samples: KX-4 rose to 0.8 mg/L at peak bloom (week 3), then declined as Vorex gave way to Pellucid organisms (week 5). Finnek mortality peaked at week 6 — one week AFTER Vorex bloom peak and during the succession transition. Toxicity alone cannot explain the mortality timing.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: KX-4 reached only 36% of the lethal threshold. Finnek mortality peaked one week after the bloom peak, during succession.

nodes.label: TEMPERATURE_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Eastern Basin Temperature Log — 12 weeks. Temperatures: 14.2°C to 19.8°C. Standard seasonal variation. No unusual cold events, no heat spikes. Temperature well within Finnek tolerance range (10-28°C).
nodes.dead_end_signal: Temperature within normal range. Not causal.

nodes.label: MICROORGANISM_SUCCESSION_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Kethara Eastern Basin Microorganism Community — 8 weeks. Week 1-4: Vorex bloom dominant (3.1M cells/mL peak). Week 5-6: Vorex declines sharply. Week 5-8: Pellucid and Strathin microorganisms become dominant. This succession pattern is unprecedented in 30 years of station records.
nodes.inspection_detail: The succession coincided with a temperature shift from 19°C to 15°C — consistent with Vorex (warm-adapted) losing competitive advantage to Pellucid/Strathin (cool-adapted). The transition altered dissolved oxygen dynamics: Vorex sustained stable oxygen; Pellucid/Strathin community consumed oxygen differently, creating fluctuations.

nodes.label: OXYGEN_DISRUPTION_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Dissolved Oxygen Profile — Eastern Basin. Weeks 1-4 (Vorex bloom): oxygen stable at 6.8-7.4 mg/L (adequate). Weeks 5-8 (succession period): oxygen fluctuating between 2.1 and 9.3 mg/L within 24-hour cycles. Finnek require 5.0-8.5 mg/L. During low phases, Finnek experience hypoxic stress.
nodes.inspection_detail: The oxygen fluctuation is characteristic of Pellucid/Strathin communities — they photosynthesize intensely during daylight (raising oxygen) then consume it rapidly at night. This creates a physiologically stressful environment for Finnek that the Vorex community did not. The fluctuation is the proximate stressor.

nodes.label: FINNEK_DIETARY_STRUCTURE
nodes.surface_text: Finnek Dietary Biology Record. Finnek feed exclusively within the structural matrix created by Vorex microorganisms — Vorex forms a layered biofilm that Finnek graze on. Pellucid and Strathin communities do not form equivalent structures. Historical record: Finnek populations collapse within 6-8 weeks of any community transition away from Vorex dominance.
nodes.inspection_detail: This record is from a 2018 station study (one of only two such transitions recorded). In both prior transition events, Finnek showed significant decline. The record confirms: community succession is the trigger, not toxin.

nodes.label: DREDGING_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Maintenance Dredging Log. Routine dredging conducted in western basin 10 weeks ago. No dredging in eastern basin in past 18 months. Dredging activity cannot explain eastern basin anomaly — wrong location.
nodes.dead_end_signal: Dredging was in the western basin, not eastern. Not causal for eastern anomaly.

nodes.label: SUCCESSION_TIMING_CORRELATION
nodes.surface_text: Comparative Succession-Mortality Timeline. Week 3: Vorex peak (KX-4: 0.8 mg/L — sub-lethal). Week 5: Succession begins. Week 5.5: Oxygen fluctuations begin. Week 6: Finnek mortality spikes. Week 8: Finnek at 19% of baseline. The succession onset (week 5) precedes mortality spike (week 6) by 1 week.
nodes.inspection_detail: The 1-week lag matches the documented Finnek physiological response time to food structure disruption: 5-8 days to show population-level mortality. The sequence is: succession → food structure loss + oxygen fluctuation → Finnek mortality. Not: bloom peak → toxin → mortality.

nodes.label: WESTERN_BASIN_COMPARISON
nodes.surface_text: Western Basin Control Data. Western basin: no Vorex bloom this season, no succession event, no microorganism community transition. Finnek population: stable at 2,300 individuals. Dissolved oxygen: stable at 7.1 mg/L.
nodes.inspection_detail: The western basin provides the control: same station, same season, no succession, no Finnek decline. The eastern-western contrast isolates the succession event as the differentiating factor.

nodes.label: NUTRIENT_LOAD_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Eastern Basin Nutrient Readings. Current phosphorus: 0.48 mg/L (historical baseline: 0.06 mg/L — 8x above normal). Nitrogen: 3.2 mg/L (historical: 0.4 mg/L — 8x above normal). Nutrient spike onset: approximately 11 weeks ago. Directional analysis: nutrients entering from the eastern margin, consistent with upstream inflow from Delvorn district.
nodes.inspection_detail: Elevated nutrients are what enabled the Vorex bloom — without excess phosphorus and nitrogen, Vorex would not have reached bloom density. The bloom then created the conditions for succession when temperature shifted. Nutrients are the root-of-chain cause.

nodes.label: DELVORN_IRRIGATION_EXPANSION
nodes.surface_text: Delvorn District Land Use Change. Records show Delvorn Irrigation Network expanded operations 14 weeks ago, converting 480 hectares of buffer marshland to irrigated cropland. Drainage from expanded irrigated area flows into Kethara eastern basin via the Kethara inlet channel. The buffer marshland previously intercepted and processed agricultural runoff.
nodes.inspection_detail: The marshland buffer removed phosphorus from agricultural drainage at approximately 85% efficiency. With the buffer gone, raw agricultural drainage now enters Kethara directly. Timeline: expansion (14 weeks ago) → nutrient spike (11 weeks ago) → Vorex bloom (weeks 2-4) → succession (weeks 5-8) → Finnek collapse (week 6+).

nodes.label: DELVORN_PERMIT_AND_TIMELINE
nodes.surface_text: Delvorn Irrigation Network Expansion Permit #KWT-2025-0044. Issued 16 weeks ago. Scope: conversion of buffer marshland Zone K-14 to irrigated agriculture. Environmental assessment: Category B (standard). The assessment did not evaluate downstream nutrient loading effects on Kethara microorganism communities or secondary succession dynamics.
nodes.inspection_detail: The permit assessment addressed direct water usage but not the loss of the buffer marshland's nutrient-filtering function. This gap was not unusual — buffer marshland nutrient processing capacity is rarely evaluated in standard agricultural expansion assessments.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Delvorn Irrigation Network's marshland conversion removed the phosphorus-filtering buffer feeding Kethara's eastern basin. Excess nutrients drove a Vorex bloom. When temperature shifted, Vorex gave way to Pellucid/Strathin communities (succession). The succession disrupted Finnek's food structure and created oxygen fluctuations. Fish died from food-structure loss and hypoxic cycles during succession — not from Vorex toxicity, which never reached lethal concentration.

correctness_tiers.full_1.0: Identifies Delvorn expansion + succession mechanism + oxygen/food disruption

correctness_tiers.partial_0.3: Identifies bloom as proximate cause without root cause
correctness_tiers.zero: Attributes to temperature, dredging, or unrelated factors

blind_framing: REGIONAL HEALTH ALERT — Tarven Province. Mortality rates in Tarven Province have increased 4.1% over the past 6 years, concentrated in districts with large livestock operations. Standard investigation teams have found no consistent cause. You have access to the linked provincial health and ecological data network. Investigate.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Vornal's patent expiry on Dextamine (livestock anti-inflammatory) → cheap generics → mass livestock use → Dextamine residue in carcasses → Graith scavenger birds ingest carcasses → Dextamine causes acute organ failure in Graith → Graith population collapses 89% → carcasses no longer cleared → Skorn canids (secondary scavengers) expand into ecological vacancy → Skorn carry Fevan virus → human-Skorn contact increases → human Fevan infection increases → mortality rises
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Functional replacement cascade — a keystone scavenger's removal allows a different species to fill the niche, but the replacement species carries additional risks the original species did not. Never named in the graph.

nodes.label: TARVEN_MORTALITY_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: TARVEN PROVINCE MORTALITY REPORT. Excess mortality (above expected baseline) in Tarven Province: +4.1% over 6 years. Spatially concentrated in districts with high livestock density. Cause: unknown. Three prior investigations: inconclusive. Investigators noted 'possible agricultural chemical exposure' and 'possible zoonotic pathway' but neither confirmed.
nodes.inspection_detail: Mortality increase began 6 years ago. The spatial pattern is tighter than expected for a diffuse cause — the effect is strongest within 15km of major livestock operations. Standard chemical panels on victims: negative for known agricultural toxins.

nodes.label: LIVESTOCK_DISTRICT_MORTALITY_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Tarven Province Mortality by District. Districts with >500 livestock/km²: average excess mortality +6.8%. Districts with 100-500 livestock/km²: +2.1%. Districts with <100 livestock/km²: +0.3%. The dose-response relationship is statistically robust (p<0.0001).
nodes.inspection_detail: The livestock density correlation seems to point toward a direct agricultural cause (exposure, chemical, or pathogen from livestock operations). However, standard occupational health screening for these populations finds no unusual chemical exposures.

nodes.label: GRAITH_BIRD_POPULATION
nodes.surface_text: Graith Scavenger Bird Population — Tarven Province. Current census: 4,100 individuals. Historical baseline (12 years ago): 37,200 individuals. Decline: 89% over 10 years. Graith are large obligate scavengers that process livestock carcasses across the region. Their decline began approximately 10 years ago — 4 years before the human mortality increase.
nodes.inspection_detail: Graith population collapse is almost entirely explained by mortality in adult birds — juveniles are still being born at normal rates. Something is killing adult Graith at the carcass sites where they feed.

nodes.label: AGRICULTURAL_CHEMICAL_INVESTIGATION
nodes.surface_text: Agricultural Chemical Screening — Tarven Province. Soil and water samples from 24 districts: standard pesticide and herbicide panels. Unusual compound detected: Dextamine metabolite (a livestock anti-inflammatory drug) in soil near carcass disposal sites. However, Dextamine metabolite in soil is well below any known human toxicity threshold. DISCONFIRMATION: Dextamine in soil cannot directly explain human mortality — it is not acutely toxic to humans at measured concentrations.
nodes.inspection_detail: Dextamine metabolite in soil means livestock are being treated with Dextamine. The compound reaches soil via carcasses. But the direct pathway (soil → human exposure) is not supported by the measured concentrations. Something else must be the pathway.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Dextamine metabolite concentrations in soil are below human toxicity thresholds. Direct exposure cannot explain 4.1% excess mortality.

nodes.label: CARCASS_ACCUMULATION_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Livestock Carcass Disposal Records — Tarven Province. Undisposed carcass density increased significantly over past 8 years — now 340% above historical baseline. Informal records from livestock operators: 'Graith birds used to clear carcasses within 48 hours. Now they take weeks, sometimes never.' Carcass accumulation sites correlate with Skorn canid den establishment.
nodes.inspection_detail: Carcasses are accumulating because Graith are no longer clearing them. Something else must be clearing or accessing the carcasses — or the carcasses are simply sitting. This creates an ecological vacancy that Skorn appear to be filling.

nodes.label: SKORN_CANID_POPULATION
nodes.surface_text: Skorn Canid Population — Tarven Province. Census from 6 years ago: 2,100 individuals. Current census: 18,400 individuals. 8.7x increase over 6 years. Expansion correlates with Graith decline and carcass accumulation. Skorn are opportunistic scavengers that have expanded into Graith's former niche.
nodes.inspection_detail: Skorn population boom began approximately 1-2 years after Graith began declining significantly. The timing matches: Graith declines → carcasses available → Skorn expand. Skorn have increased human-contact rates as their population density increased near livestock operations.

nodes.label: GRAITH_TISSUE_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Graith Carcass Tissue Analysis — 47 specimens. All specimens: Dextamine compound detected in organ tissue at concentrations consistent with ingestion via contaminated livestock carcasses. Pathological findings: acute visceral failure — same mechanism documented in laboratory tests when Graith-type birds are exposed to Dextamine at carcass-equivalent concentrations.
nodes.inspection_detail: Dextamine is not toxic to humans at environmental concentrations, but Graith are highly sensitive to it — the compound causes acute organ failure in Graith at doses absorbed through carcass consumption. Dextamine was not widely used until generic versions became available 10 years ago (after original patent expired).

nodes.label: GRAITH_MORTALITY_CAUSE
nodes.surface_text: Graith Mortality Investigation. Mortality concentrated in adults at carcass sites — exactly where Dextamine-contaminated carcasses accumulate. Juvenile mortality rates: normal. The pattern is consistent with adults dying from what they eat at carcass sites, not from disease or predation.
nodes.inspection_detail: This eliminates disease and predation as causes. The carcass-site concentration of adult mortality is the diagnostic signature of a dietary toxin.

nodes.label: NICHE_REPLACEMENT_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Ecological Niche Transition Record. Graith historically occupied the obligate scavenger niche in Tarven Province, processing 94% of large livestock carcasses within 48 hours. As Graith declined, Skorn expanded into the same carcass sites. Skorn processing rate: slower and less complete. Current carcass clearance: approximately 55% efficiency versus 94% under Graith dominance.
nodes.inspection_detail: The niche transition is now well-documented. The key consequence: Skorn are the replacement species, but Skorn carry Fevan virus — which Graith did not. The replacement changed not just the carcass clearance rate but the disease ecology of the scavenger niche.

nodes.label: SKORN_FEVAN_CARRIER_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Skorn Fevan Virus Carrier Survey. Fevan virus prevalence in Skorn: 34% of individuals tested carry active Fevan. Fevan is a zoonotic virus transmissible to humans through direct contact, bite wounds, or contaminated carcass handling. Human Fevan cases in Tarven Province: increasing year-over-year since Skorn population expansion.
nodes.inspection_detail: Fevan is the human mortality mechanism. Skorn carry it. As Skorn density increased near livestock operations, human-Skorn contact rates increased, and Fevan transmission increased.

nodes.label: HUMAN_SKORN_CONTACT_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Human-Skorn Contact Records. Skorn contact incidents per 1,000 farm workers: Year 1 (6 years ago): 4.2/year. Year 6 (current): 31.7/year. Increase: 7.5x. Temporal pattern: contact rates began rising 1-2 years after Skorn population expansion, as Skorn began colonizing farmyard areas previously less frequented.
nodes.inspection_detail: The contact rate increase is consistent with population density dynamics — more Skorn means more encounters. The 7.5x increase in contact, combined with 34% Fevan carriage, produces the transmission signal in the mortality data.

nodes.label: FEVAN_MORTALITY_CONFIRMATION
nodes.surface_text: Fevan Cause-of-Death Analysis. Post-mortem analysis of 84 excess mortality cases in high-livestock districts: 71 (85%) show Fevan antibodies or active Fevan infection at time of death. Fevan was not previously a significant cause of death in Tarven Province — historical rate was approximately 0.2% of deaths. Current rate in affected districts: approximately 4.8% of deaths.
nodes.inspection_detail: Fevan is confirmed as the proximate human mortality cause. The chain is: Dextamine → Graith collapse → Skorn expansion → Fevan transmission → human deaths.

nodes.label: DEXTAMINE_GENERIC_ENTRY_TIMELINE
nodes.surface_text: Dextamine Pharmaceutical History. Original Dextamine patent held by Vornal Pharmaceutical Group, expired 11 years ago. Generic Dextamine entered the market 10 years ago at 40% of original price. Livestock use of Dextamine in Tarven Province increased 380% following generic entry. Current Dextamine tissue residue levels in livestock carcasses: 4.2x higher than pre-generic era.
nodes.inspection_detail: The patent expiry → generic entry → mass livestock use → increased carcass contamination → Graith exposure increase timeline is the root of the entire chain. Vornal's original patent is the structural origin, but the operational cause is the mass adoption of cheap generics.

nodes.label: VORNAL_PHARMACEUTICAL_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Vornal Pharmaceutical Group — Dextamine Product Record. Dextamine (livestock anti-inflammatory): approved 22 years ago, patent expired 11 years ago. Vornal no longer controls pricing. Post-generic adoption rate in livestock operations: widespread. Vornal is named as the compound originator in all pharmacological records for Dextamine.
nodes.inspection_detail: Vornal cannot be held directly responsible for generic manufacturers' behavior — their patent rights have expired. However, the compound's design (highly effective anti-inflammatory, inadequate screening for scavenger bird toxicity at the time of approval) created the latent pathway. The approval did not require testing on obligate scavenger species.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Vornal Pharmaceutical Group's Dextamine compound, adopted massively following patent expiry, contaminated livestock carcasses. Graith scavengers that consumed the carcasses developed acute organ failure from Dextamine. Graith collapsed 89%. Skorn canids expanded into the vacant scavenger niche. Skorn carry Fevan virus, which Graith did not. Increased Skorn density → increased human-Skorn contact → increased Fevan transmission → 4.1% excess human mortality.

blind_framing: COASTAL RESEARCH ALERT — Merantha Atoll Research Station. Reef fish biomass in the Merantha lagoon system has declined 52% over 7 years. Standard reef health monitoring shows coral cover normal. Marine stressor investigations have been inconclusive. You have access to the station's linked ecological data network.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Pelk mammals were introduced to Merantha's terrestrial islands. Pelk predate on Threnn seabirds. Threnn collapsed 78%. Threnn deposited guano that provided nitrogen to the Merantha lagoon reef system. Without Threnn guano, nitrogen input to the reef dropped 67%. Reef fish that depended on nitrogen-subsidized productivity declined 52%. The cause is terrestrial-to-marine via nutrient flow, invisible to standard marine monitoring.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Subsidized ecosystem collapse — one ecosystem (terrestrial island) indirectly sustains another (coral reef) through nutrient flow. Removing the connector (seabird) starves the downstream ecosystem. The causal path crosses an ecosystem boundary. Never named in the graph.

nodes.label: MERANTHA_REEF_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: Merantha Atoll Lagoon — 7-Year Trend Report. Reef fish biomass decline: 52%. Coral cover: stable at 68% (within normal historical range). Standard marine stressor index: LOW. Temperature anomalies: none significant. Fishing pressure index: unchanged. Station director: 'We cannot explain this decline. Marine indicators are all normal or within range.'
nodes.inspection_detail: The decline is spatially uniform across the lagoon, not patchy as expected from bleaching or localized overfishing. The decline is also gradual — consistent with a slow, sustained reduction in productivity rather than an acute event.

nodes.label: REEF_FISH_BIOMASS_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Reef Fish Species Census — Merantha Lagoon. Total biomass: 52% of 7-year baseline. Most affected species: Kelvin reef fish (nitrogen-sensitive productivity feeders). Least affected: Poran reef fish (low nitrogen requirement). The species-specific pattern suggests a nutrient limitation mechanism.
nodes.inspection_detail: Kelvin fish are disproportionately affected. Kelvin are nitrogen-sensitive — they depend on a productivity regime fueled by high nitrogen availability. Poran fish (which persist) have lower nitrogen requirements. This differential impact is an early signal that nitrogen might be the limiting factor.

nodes.label: MARINE_STRESSOR_INVESTIGATION
nodes.surface_text: Standard Marine Stressor Report — Merantha. Bleaching events: none in survey period. Sedimentation: within historical range. Fishing effort: no significant change. Ocean temperature: within ±0.8°C of historical mean. Water chemistry (pH, salinity): normal. DISCONFIRMATION: All standard marine stressors are within normal ranges. The decline cannot be explained by any documented marine stressor.
nodes.inspection_detail: This comprehensive marine stressor assessment covers all known drivers of reef fish decline. None are elevated. The anomaly must have an unconventional explanation — possibly from outside the marine monitoring framework entirely.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: All marine stressors within normal range. Requires pivot to non-standard causal pathways.

nodes.label: FISHING_PRESSURE_RECORDS
nodes.surface_text: Merantha Fishing Activity Log. Licensed fishing vessels: 14 (unchanged for 9 years). Total catch weight: stable. No poaching reports in 7 years. Fishing cannot explain a 52% decline.

nodes.label: BLEACHING_HYPOTHESIS
nodes.surface_text: Coral Bleaching Investigation. Coral cover: 68% — within historical range. Bleaching incidents: none recorded. Symbiodinium density in coral tissue: normal. No heat stress accumulation recorded in thermal monitoring. DISCONFIRMATION: Coral health is normal. Bleaching cannot explain fish decline when coral is healthy.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Coral is healthy. Fish decline is not habitat loss from bleaching.

nodes.label: LAGOON_NITROGEN_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Merantha Lagoon Nutrient Profile — 10-Year Record. Dissolved inorganic nitrogen: currently 0.31 µmol/L. Historical baseline (year 1-3 of record): 0.94 µmol/L. Decline: 67% over 7 years. Nitrogen declined steadily over the same period as reef fish biomass.
nodes.inspection_detail: The nitrogen decline is highly correlated with the fish biomass decline (r=0.91 over 7 years). Reef fish productivity in nitrogen-poor systems declines proportionally. But the source of the nitrogen decline is unknown — external nutrient inputs have not been measured before.

nodes.label: NITROGEN_ISOTOPE_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Nitrogen Source Isotopic Analysis — Merantha Lagoon. δ15N (nitrogen isotope ratio) analysis of lagoon sediments and water: historical sediments show a distinct seabird-guano nitrogen signature (δ15N ≈ +11‰). Recent sediments (past 7 years): δ15N declining toward open-ocean values (+6‰). The seabird-derived nitrogen contribution has decreased dramatically.
nodes.inspection_detail: Isotopic analysis is the key: the lagoon's historical nitrogen was predominantly seabird-guano derived. Guano-derived nitrogen has a distinctive isotopic signature — it cannot be mistaken for other sources. This confirms that seabird inputs supported the lagoon's nutrient regime.

nodes.label: THRENN_SEABIRD_POPULATION
nodes.surface_text: Threnn Seabird Colony — Merantha Islands. Current nesting pairs: 1,240. Historical baseline (10 years ago): 5,600 nesting pairs. Decline: 78% over 8 years. Threnn are colonial seabirds that nest on Merantha's terrestrial islands and feed in the surrounding ocean. They defecate on the islands and near-shore areas, delivering marine-derived nitrogen to the lagoon system.
nodes.inspection_detail: Threnn decline began approximately 8 years ago — 1 year before the reef fish decline became detectable. Their guano historically delivered an estimated 4.2 kg nitrogen/m²/year to the island areas draining to the lagoon. Current contribution: approximately 0.9 kg/m²/year.

nodes.label: SPATIAL_ISLAND_COMPARISON
nodes.surface_text: Comparative Analysis — Islands With vs. Without Pelk Mammals. Merantha North Island (Pelk-free): Threnn colony intact (4,100 pairs), lagoon nitrogen: 0.82 µmol/L, reef fish biomass: -8% (minor decline). Merantha South Island (Pelk present): Threnn colony collapsed to 210 pairs, lagoon nitrogen: 0.19 µmol/L, reef fish biomass: -71%. The spatial pattern is stark.
nodes.inspection_detail: This comparison provides the critical spatial control: identical reef systems, different outcomes based solely on whether Pelk mammals were present on the adjacent island. Pelk presence → Threnn collapse → nitrogen decline → fish decline.

nodes.label: GUANO_DEPOSITION_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Guano Deposition Monitoring — Merantha Islands. Annual guano nitrogen delivery to lagoon-draining areas: Year 1: 4.1 kg/m²/year. Year 4: 2.8 kg/m²/year. Year 7 (current): 0.9 kg/m²/year. The decline in guano input (78% reduction) matches the Threnn population decline (78%) and precedes the reef fish decline by approximately 1-2 years.
nodes.inspection_detail: The guano input decline lag before fish decline is consistent with nutrient dynamics: changes in nutrient input take 1-2 years to manifest in reef fish productivity. The causal sequence is confirmed by timing.

nodes.label: PELK_PREDATION_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Pelk Mammal Predation Records — Merantha Islands. Pelk mammals (introduced approximately 9 years ago as part of a land management program) prey on ground-nesting and cliff-nesting birds. Pelk predation rate on Threnn nests: 71% of nests disturbed or destroyed annually. Population modeling: at current Pelk density, Threnn cannot maintain population positive growth rate.
nodes.inspection_detail: Pelk were introduced for vegetation management without a full assessment of their impacts on seabird colonies. Their predation on Threnn is direct and severe — Threnn cannot reproduce fast enough to compensate for nest predation at current Pelk density.

nodes.label: HISTORICAL_THRENN_NITROGEN_CORRELATION
nodes.surface_text: Historical Record — Threnn Colony Size vs. Lagoon Productivity (12-year dataset). Years 1-5: Threnn at full population, lagoon nitrogen high, fish biomass high. Years 6-8: Threnn declining, nitrogen declining 1-2 years later, fish biomass declining 2-3 years later. The relationship is present in the historical record, not just in current data.
nodes.inspection_detail: This historical correlation confirms the pattern is not coincidental. The Threnn-nitrogen-fish relationship has been consistent across multiple years, producing an associative learning signal: lower Threnn → lower nitrogen → lower fish, repeatedly across the time series.

nodes.label: PELK_INTRODUCTION_PERMIT
nodes.surface_text: Pelk Mammal Introduction Record — Merantha Island Management Program. Program initiated 9 years ago. Objective: invasive vegetation control. Pelk introduced under Merantha Regional Conservation Authority permit #MC-2016-088. Environmental assessment: evaluated vegetation impacts only. Seabird impacts: not assessed. Marine ecosystem downstream effects: not assessed.
nodes.inspection_detail: The introduction decision was made without assessing cross-ecosystem effects. The concept of guano-nitrogen subsidies to adjacent marine systems was not within the assessment scope. The causal pathway ran through an ecosystem boundary invisible to the conventional assessment framework.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Pelk mammals introduced to Merantha islands under a vegetation management program destroyed 78% of the Threnn seabird colony through nest predation. Threnn historically provided guano-derived nitrogen subsidies to the adjacent lagoon reef system. Nitrogen input declined 67%, reducing reef productivity and causing 52% fish biomass decline. Standard marine monitoring could not detect this cause because it originated in the terrestrial ecosystem.

blind_framing: FIELD STATION ALERT — Dovren Reserve Vegetation Survey. Native plant diversity in Dovren Reserve has declined 44% over 5 years. An invasive predator (Lurrek) was introduced approximately 6 years ago. Initial investigation concluded Lurrek has no significant impact because Lurrek predation rates appear moderate. Station ecologist requests a second investigation.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Lurrek (invasive predator) introduced → Dovren Grazer (native herbivore) detects Lurrek presence → Grazer shifts from preferred plant species (Delvite) to alternative species (Rennox, Corthal) in Lurrek-free refuges → Delvite, no longer being grazed, grows unchecked and outcompetes other native plants → native plant diversity collapses. The mechanism is behavioral (foraging shift), not demographic (population decline). Grazer population counts are NORMAL — which is the disconfirmation.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Trait-mediated indirect interaction — the invasive species changes a native species' behavior (not its population), and the behavioral change cascades to affect plant communities. Never named in the graph.

nodes.label: DOVREN_DIVERSITY_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: Dovren Reserve Vegetation Survey. Native plant species count: 47 species (5-year baseline: 84 species). Decline: 44%. Most affected: Delvite plant (present 5 years ago, now <5% cover). Least affected: Rennox and Corthal plants (still widespread). Lurrek invasive predator introduced 6 years ago. Prior investigation: 'Lurrek predation on Dovren Grazers is moderate (12% annual take). Not sufficient to explain plant decline.'
nodes.inspection_detail: The plant diversity collapse is not uniform — some plants (Rennox, Corthal) persist while others (Delvite and its associated species) decline. This compositional change suggests a species-specific mechanism rather than a general stressor.

nodes.label: PLANT_DECLINE_COMPOSITION
nodes.surface_text: Plant Species Change Record — 5 Years. Species lost or critically reduced (>80% cover loss): Delvite, Vernt herb, Sellock grass. Species stable or increasing: Rennox shrub, Corthal vine. Historical grazing preference data: Dovren Grazers heavily preferred Delvite and Vernt herb (78% of diet). Rennox and Corthal: low grazing preference (8% of diet combined).
nodes.inspection_detail: The species being lost are exactly those that Grazers prefer. The species persisting are those Grazers rarely ate. This pattern is consistent with either (a) Grazers eating more of preferred species (increased pressure) OR (b) preferred species losing their competitive protection from Grazer activity.

nodes.label: GRAZER_POPULATION_CHECK
nodes.surface_text: Dovren Grazer Population Census. Current count: 847 individuals. 5-year baseline: 831 individuals. Change: +2% (effectively stable). DISCONFIRMATION: Grazer population has NOT declined. If Lurrek were reducing Grazers enough to release plants from grazing pressure, there would be fewer Grazers. Grazer numbers are normal.
nodes.inspection_detail: Population models indicate that a 12% annual predation take by Lurrek is offset by normal Grazer reproduction. The Grazer population is stable. This rules out the hypothesis that fewer Grazers explain the plant decline. Something must be different about WHAT the Grazers are doing, not HOW MANY there are.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Grazer population is STABLE (+2%). The mechanism cannot be reduced grazing from fewer Grazers.

nodes.label: LURREK_DIRECT_PREDATION_RATE
nodes.surface_text: Lurrek Predation Rate Assessment. Annual predation on Dovren Grazers: 12% of population. This is within the range that prior assessments classified as 'moderate' and consistent with Grazer reproductive compensation. DISCONFIRMATION: Direct Lurrek predation is insufficient to drive plant community collapse at this rate — the population model confirms Grazers can absorb this predation rate without significant decline.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: 12% annual predation rate is within compensation capacity. Not sufficient to cause indirect plant effects through Grazer population decline.

nodes.label: LURREK_PRESENCE_MAP
nodes.surface_text: Lurrek Distribution — Dovren Reserve. Lurrek occupy approximately 65% of the reserve, concentrated in open habitats. Lurrek-free areas: dense woodland patches and steep rocky outcrops (approximately 35% of reserve area). These Lurrek-free areas have become the primary Grazer refuge zones.
nodes.inspection_detail: The spatial distribution creates a landscape partitioning: Lurrek-present areas are now high-predation-risk zones for Grazers. Grazers avoid these areas for foraging.

nodes.label: GRAZER_FORAGING_SHIFT_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Dovren Grazer Foraging Behavioral Study. GPS tracking of 40 Grazers across 2 years. Result: Grazers have fundamentally shifted their foraging geography. Pre-Lurrek historical areas (Delvite-rich open zones): now used for only 18% of foraging time (historical: 71%). Lurrek-free refuge zones: now used for 74% of foraging time. Diet analysis in refuge zones: Rennox and Corthal are the dominant plants there. Delvite is rare in refuge zones.
nodes.inspection_detail: Grazers are still eating — but they are eating different plants in different places. Their total foraging time and caloric intake are normal. The shift is behavioral, not driven by population change. In avoiding Lurrek, they have abandoned the Delvite-rich open zones.

nodes.label: REFUGE_ZONE_PLANT_STATUS
nodes.surface_text: Refuge Zone Vegetation Survey. Lurrek-free refuge zones: Rennox and Corthal cover high. Delvite: present at 67% cover (healthy). Refuge zones show minimal plant diversity decline. COMPARISON: Open zones (Lurrek present): Delvite cover collapsed to <5%, native diversity loss >60%.
nodes.inspection_detail: The refuge zones provide the spatial control: same reserve, no Lurrek, Grazers now present and grazing Rennox/Corthal — and Delvite is THRIVING. In open zones without Grazers (they've moved to refuges), Delvite has no competition control and has been outcompeted by other species.

nodes.label: DELVITE_COMPETITIVE_RELEASE
nodes.surface_text: Delvite Competitive Dynamics Record. Delvite is a weak competitor against Rennox and Corthal under normal conditions. Historical maintenance of Delvite at high cover was entirely dependent on Grazer preferential grazing — Grazers kept Rennox and Corthal suppressed, maintaining open space for Delvite. Without Grazer foraging in open zones, Rennox and Corthal now out-compete Delvite. Delvite has no competitive defense without Grazer activity.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is the mechanism: Delvite depended on Grazers to suppress its competitors. When Grazers relocated to refuges (to avoid Lurrek), they stopped suppressing Rennox/Corthal in the open zones. Rennox/Corthal expanded. Delvite collapsed. The plant that was lost is the one that depended on the herbivore it could no longer attract.

nodes.label: DELVITE_HISTORICAL_GRAZER_RELATIONSHIP
nodes.surface_text: Historical Record — Delvite and Grazer Interaction (12-year dataset). Years 1-6 (pre-Lurrek): Grazers heavily forage open zones, Delvite maintains 68% cover, Rennox/Corthal suppressed at <15% cover. Years 7-12 (Lurrek present): Grazer foraging in open zones declining year-by-year, Delvite declining year-by-year in correlation. The relationship is consistent across the historical record.
nodes.inspection_detail: This provides the associative learning signal: declining Grazer open-zone use correlates with declining Delvite across 6 years. The pattern predates the current investigation and is consistent with a sustained mechanism.

nodes.label: ANTI_PREDATOR_BEHAVIOR_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Grazer Anti-Predator Behavior Study. Field observations and experimental Lurrek-scent studies: Grazers show strong aversion to Lurrek-scent-marked areas. Even in Lurrek-free patches within Lurrek-dominated habitat, Grazers avoid foraging if Lurrek scent is present within 200 meters. The behavioral response is well-documented and consistent: Grazers relocate foraging, they do not reduce foraging time.
nodes.inspection_detail: The key finding: Grazers' total food intake is maintained — they eat the same amount, just in different places. The behavioral avoidance is spatial, not metabolic. Lurrek's effect is through fear, not through actually killing enough Grazers to matter.

nodes.label: CLIMATE_CHECK
nodes.surface_text: Dovren Reserve Climate Record — 6 Years. Rainfall: within ±11% of historical mean. Temperature: within ±0.9°C. No drought events. No frost events. Climate cannot explain species-specific plant decline pattern.
nodes.dead_end_signal: Climate within normal range. Not causal.

nodes.label: LURREK_INTRODUCTION_ASSESSMENT_GAP
nodes.surface_text: Lurrek Introduction Environmental Assessment — Dovren Reserve Management Record. Lurrek introduced 6 years ago under a predator reintroduction program. Environmental assessment: evaluated direct predation on native species. Did NOT evaluate: indirect effects of fear-induced foraging shifts on plant community dynamics. Assessment conclusion: 'Moderate predation pressure expected, within tolerable limits.' The behavioral cascade pathway was not within the assessment framework.
nodes.inspection_detail: The assessment correctly predicted direct predation rates. It failed to predict that Grazers would behaviorally relocate and thereby release competitive pressure on plants they previously suppressed. This class of indirect effect — behavior-mediated, not population-mediated — was outside standard impact assessment methodology.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Lurrek introduction. Lurrek's predation risk caused Dovren Grazers to behaviorally relocate foraging to predator-free refuge zones — without reducing their population. In refuge zones, Grazers now eat Rennox and Corthal, which were previously suppressed. In the abandoned open zones, Rennox/Corthal expanded unchecked, outcompeting Delvite and 43 other native species. The mechanism is behavioral, not demographic — Grazer population is stable but Grazer foraging geography changed entirely.

blind_framing: LAKE MANAGEMENT ALERT — Ostren Regional Water Authority. The Corvel Lake restoration program has been operating for 5 years. Phosphorus reduction targets have been met. Corvel Lake should be recovering. It is not. Algal blooms continue at pre-program levels. The Water Authority has requested an independent investigation of why the program is failing.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Decades of agricultural runoff deposited phosphorus in Corvel Lake sediments. External phosphorus reduction program correctly reduced new inputs. However, during warm months, thermal stratification creates anoxic conditions in deep water. Anoxic conditions chemically release phosphorus from sediments (internal loading). Sediment-released phosphorus now exceeds external inputs by 3:1. The program addressed the wrong source — the dominant source had already shifted from external to internal.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Historical debt mechanism — past damage creates a stored internal source that outlasts external source reduction. Temporal shift in dominant cause. Never named in the graph.

nodes.label: CORVEL_PROGRAM_FAILURE_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: Corvel Lake Restoration Program — Year 5 Assessment. External phosphorus inputs: reduced 74% (target: 70%). Program compliance: ACHIEVED. Algal bloom frequency: unchanged from pre-program baseline. Bloom intensity: unchanged. Corvel Lake is showing no water quality improvement despite successful external phosphorus reduction.
nodes.inspection_detail: The program by all standard measures is working — inputs are down, compliance is high. But the lake is not responding. This is paradoxical. Either the measurement is wrong, the compliance is wrong, or the program is targeting the wrong thing.

nodes.label: BLOOM_DATA_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Corvel Lake Algal Bloom Record — 10 Years. Pre-program (years 1-5): average 4.2 major bloom events per year, peak chlorophyll a: 180 µg/L. During program (years 6-10): average 4.1 major bloom events per year, peak chlorophyll a: 174 µg/L. No statistically significant improvement despite 5 years of external phosphorus reduction.
nodes.inspection_detail: The bloom data shows the lake is not responding. This is increasingly rare in restoration literature — external phosphorus reduction almost always produces some improvement in 5 years. Something is sustaining the phosphorus availability independent of external inputs.

nodes.label: COMPLIANCE_VERIFICATION
nodes.surface_text: External Phosphorus Reduction — Compliance Audit. Independent verification of all upstream sources: agricultural operations in compliance with nutrient management plans (97% compliance). Wastewater treatment plant: phosphorus effluent meeting standards. Stormwater: no exceedances detected. Total measured external phosphorus input: 1.8 tonnes/year. Pre-program baseline: 6.9 tonnes/year. REDUCTION CONFIRMED. DISCONFIRMATION: External reduction is real and significant. The program is not failing due to non-compliance.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: External phosphorus reduced 74% — verified independently. Program is compliant. The bloom persistence cannot be explained by non-compliance.

nodes.label: UPSTREAM_SOURCE_INVESTIGATION
nodes.surface_text: Upstream Phosphorus Source Survey — Corvel Watershed. All known point sources (wastewater, industrial): compliant. Agricultural non-point sources: reduced to target levels. Mystery check: is there an unidentified new source? Survey finds no new discharge permits, no unauthorized dumping. External inputs are genuinely reduced.
nodes.dead_end_signal: No new external sources found. External loading is genuinely reduced.

nodes.label: TRIBUTARY_LOADING_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Tributary Monitoring Data — 5 Years. All tributaries entering Corvel Lake monitored continuously. Total external loading trend: declining year-over-year. Year 10 loading: 1.8 tonnes/year (74% below baseline). No tributary shows anomalous increases. External source picture is complete and confirms compliance.

nodes.label: PHOSPHORUS_BUDGET_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Corvel Lake Total Phosphorus Budget — Current Year. External inputs: 1.8 tonnes/year. Internal loading (measured from sediment flux studies): 5.4 tonnes/year. Total phosphorus entering the lake water column: 7.2 tonnes/year. External reduction success reduced external inputs by 5.1 tonnes/year — but internal loading has INCREASED by 3.8 tonnes/year over the same period. Net phosphorus reduction: minimal.
nodes.inspection_detail: The phosphorus budget is the key revelation. External phosphorus was reduced 74% (from 6.9 to 1.8 tonnes). But internal sediment loading, which was 1.6 tonnes/year pre-program, is now 5.4 tonnes/year — it has tripled. The program saved 5.1 tonnes externally while losing 3.8 tonnes internally. Net improvement: 1.3 tonnes (18% of pre-program total).

nodes.label: BLOOM_SEASONAL_PATTERN
nodes.surface_text: Bloom Seasonality Record. Corvel Lake blooms: 90% of bloom events occur in June-September (warm months). Bloom events: virtually absent in November-March (cool months). The seasonal pattern is tightly linked to warm-season stratification — when the lake thermally stratifies, blooms follow.
nodes.inspection_detail: The warm-season bloom concentration is a clue: thermal stratification occurs in warm months, creating anoxic bottom conditions. Anoxic conditions drive sediment phosphorus release. The seasonal pattern is consistent with internal loading being the dominant driver.

nodes.label: INTERNAL_LOADING_MECHANISM
nodes.surface_text: Sediment Phosphorus Release Study. When Corvel Lake thermally stratifies (summer), the hypolimnion (deep water) becomes anoxic (oxygen-depleted). Under anoxic conditions, iron-phosphorus bonds in sediments dissolve, releasing phosphorus into the water column. Release rate under anoxic conditions: 4.8 mg P/m²/day. Lake bottom area: 430 hectares. Total summer release: approximately 5.1 tonnes/year.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is the mechanism. The sediments accumulated phosphorus over decades. When the lake stratifies and the hypolimnion goes anoxic, that accumulated phosphorus is chemically released. The lake 'remembers' decades of loading even after external inputs are stopped.

nodes.label: SEDIMENT_CORE_HISTORY
nodes.surface_text: Corvel Lake Sediment Core Analysis. Cores extracted from multiple lake zones. Historical phosphorus accumulation record: phosphorus concentration in sediments increasing from pre-agricultural era (0.3 mg P/g dry sediment) to current (4.2 mg P/g dry sediment) — a 14-fold increase over 80 years. Sediment phosphorus inventory: approximately 840 tonnes stored in top 20cm of sediments.
nodes.inspection_detail: 840 tonnes of phosphorus stored in sediments — even at 5 tonnes/year internal release, this represents 168 years of potential continued loading. External source reduction cannot address this stockpile. The historical debt is orders of magnitude larger than annual inputs.

nodes.label: THERMAL_STRATIFICATION_TREND
nodes.surface_text: Thermal Stratification Duration — 30-Year Record. Days per year with complete thermal stratification: 30 years ago: 71 days. 10 years ago: 89 days. Current: 106 days. Climate warming has extended the stratification season, extending the anoxic window and increasing internal loading beyond historical rates.
nodes.inspection_detail: This explains why internal loading INCREASED during the program years (even as external loads fell): warming extended the anoxic season, releasing more sediment phosphorus each year. The program reduced external inputs while the internal source was getting worse due to an unrelated driver (warming).

nodes.label: ANOXIA_DEPTH_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Hypolimnion Anoxia Record — Current Year. Anoxic conditions (oxygen <2 mg/L) measured in deep water: present from week 22 to week 42 (20 weeks). This anoxic period drives continuous phosphorus release from sediments throughout summer. Oxygen in upper water layers: normal (supports bloom formation).

nodes.label: PRE_PROGRAM_INTERNAL_LOADING_COMPARISON
nodes.surface_text: Internal Loading Historical Comparison. Pre-program era (year 1): internal loading estimated at 1.6 tonnes/year (based on shorter stratification season, less accumulated sediment). Current: 5.4 tonnes/year. The internal source grew by 237% during the exact period when the external source was being reduced.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is the associative learning evidence: as the program reduced external loading, internal loading increased. The two trends moved in opposite directions. The program created the impression of success (external loads down) while the actual delivery of phosphorus to the lake continued growing.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Legacy sediment phosphorus accumulated over 80 years of agricultural loading. The restoration program successfully reduced external inputs (74% reduction, verified). However, thermal stratification creates anoxic conditions in the hypolimnion, chemically releasing stored sediment phosphorus into the water column at a rate 3x higher than current external inputs. Internal loading now dominates and has increased as climate warming extended the stratification season. The program addressed the wrong phosphorus source — the dominant source had already shifted from external (current) to internal (historical).

correctness_tiers.partial_0.3: Program failure acknowledged but external source non-compliance blamed

blind_framing: FIELD STATION ALERT — Arveth Freshwater Reserve. Three specialist fish species (Plenk, Verdin, Cort) that coexisted in Arveth Reservoir for 40 years have declined dramatically over 3 years. The dominant species, Stalven, appears stable by population count. No disease has been identified. No obvious environmental stressor detected. Station ecologist requests investigation.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Upstream temperature increase caused the Stalven fish population to produce more juveniles that survive to adulthood (higher juvenile survival rate → increased Stalven juvenile density). Stalven juveniles share the juvenile resource zone with juvenile Plenk, Verdin, and Cort. Previously, Stalven juvenile density was low enough that all species coexisted. Now, Stalven juvenile abundance is so high that shared juvenile resources are over-exploited. Plenk, Verdin, Cort juveniles fail to reach adulthood in sufficient numbers. The mechanism runs through LIFE STAGE COMPOSITION (size structure), not TOTAL POPULATION.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Ontogenetic cascade — changes in the life stage composition (specifically juvenile density) of one species can be more ecologically disruptive than changes in total abundance. The dominant species' total count is stable but its size structure has shifted. Never named in the graph.

relation_types.shared_habitat_use_between: Documented overlap in habitat or resource use between entities A and B
relation_types.environmental_record_at: Physical parameter data for location A

nodes.label: ARVETH_COLLAPSE_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: Arveth Reservoir Species Alert. Plenk fish: current count 410, 3-year baseline 3,200 (-87%). Verdin fish: current count 280, baseline 2,100 (-87%). Cort fish: current count 190, baseline 1,600 (-88%). Stalven fish: current count 28,400, baseline 27,800 (+2%). All four species have coexisted for 40 years. No disease detected. No pollution anomaly detected.
nodes.inspection_detail: The three declining species are all specialist feeders in Arveth. Stalven is a generalist. The pattern — three specialists declining while a generalist is stable — could suggest competitive exclusion, but Stalven total numbers haven't changed.

nodes.label: DISEASE_SCREENING
nodes.surface_text: Disease and Pathogen Screening. All four species screened across 3 years. Result: no significant pathogen detected in any species. No evidence of disease-driven mortality in Plenk, Verdin, or Cort populations.

nodes.label: STALVEN_TOTAL_POPULATION_CHECK
nodes.surface_text: Stalven Population Detailed Census. Total count: stable at 27,800-28,400. HOWEVER — age structure analysis reveals a shift: juvenile Stalven (0-1 year class) as proportion of total population: 3 years ago: 18%. Current: 43%. Adult Stalven count: declined from 22,700 to 16,200. Juvenile Stalven count: increased from 5,100 to 12,200. DISCONFIRMATION: Stalven TOTAL count is stable, but the population is now 43% juveniles vs. 18% — a massive life-stage composition shift.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Stalven total population appears stable (+2%). But age structure has shifted dramatically — juvenile proportion doubled. The juveniles are the ecologically relevant variable.

nodes.label: WATER_QUALITY_CHECK
nodes.surface_text: Arveth Reservoir Water Quality Record — 3 Years. Dissolved oxygen: within normal range. pH: stable. Conductivity: normal. Nitrogen/phosphorus: no significant change. No chemical stressor detected.

nodes.label: STALVEN_AGE_STRUCTURE_SHIFT
nodes.surface_text: Stalven Age Class Distribution — Annual Tracking. 3-year trend: Each year, juvenile survival rate (proportion reaching 1+ year) increasing. Year 1: 24% juvenile survival. Year 2: 31%. Year 3: 38%. Historical baseline: 18-22%. More juveniles surviving means more juveniles competing in the juvenile habitat zone each year.
nodes.inspection_detail: The increased juvenile survival is the mechanistic input. Stalven juveniles compete with Plenk, Verdin, and Cort juveniles for the same resources in the same habitat zone. When Stalven juvenile density was low, coexistence was possible. At 43% juvenile proportion, Stalven juveniles dominate the shared juvenile zone.

nodes.label: PLENK_MORTALITY_STAGE_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Plenk Mortality Analysis. Population modeling using 3 years of cohort tracking: adult Plenk mortality rate: within normal range. Plenk juvenile survival rate (0 to 1 year class): 3-year baseline: 41%. Year 2: 28%. Year 3: 14%. Plenk are dying at the juvenile stage — adult mortality is normal. The population is failing to recruit new adults.
nodes.inspection_detail: Plenk are not being killed as adults. They are failing to reach adulthood. Something in the juvenile environment is preventing Plenk from completing their transition to the adult stage. The same analysis for Verdin and Cort: identical pattern — adult survival normal, juvenile survival collapsing.

nodes.label: SPECIALIST_ADULT_SURVIVAL
nodes.surface_text: Adult Survival Rates — All Species. Plenk adult survival: 88% per year (historical: 89%). Verdin: 85% (historical: 86%). Cort: 91% (historical: 90%). Adult survival rates for all three specialist species are UNCHANGED. The problem is entirely in juvenile recruitment, not adult survival.
nodes.inspection_detail: This confirms the life-stage specificity: adult survival is fine. The collapse is entirely at the juvenile → adult transition. Something is happening in the juvenile habitat zone specifically.

nodes.label: JUVENILE_HABITAT_OVERLAP
nodes.surface_text: Juvenile Habitat Use Survey — All Four Species. Juvenile habitat mapping: all four species' juveniles use the same shallow, structured habitat zone in Arveth Reservoir (eastern littoral zone). Resource partitioning by adults: well-established. Resource partitioning by juveniles: minimal — all juveniles compete for the same invertebrate food items in the same zone.
nodes.inspection_detail: Adults of all four species are ecologically separated. Juveniles are not. The competitive exclusion — if it's happening — must be occurring in the juvenile stage, where all species overlap completely.

nodes.label: JUVENILE_COMPETITION_EXPERIMENT
nodes.surface_text: Field Experiment — Juvenile Competition. Enclosure experiments varying Stalven juvenile density: at historical Stalven juvenile densities (18% of population), Plenk/Verdin/Cort juvenile survival normal (38-42%). At current Stalven juvenile densities (43%), Plenk/Verdin/Cort juvenile survival: 12-16%. The causal relationship between Stalven juvenile density and specialist juvenile survival is confirmed experimentally.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is the definitive mechanistic confirmation: Stalven juveniles competitively exclude specialist juveniles when present at current density. The exclusion is density-dependent — it didn't happen at historical Stalven juvenile densities. The key variable is not 'Stalven presence' but 'Stalven juvenile density.'

nodes.label: SPECIALIST_JUVENILE_HISTORICAL
nodes.surface_text: Historical Juvenile Survival Record — 10 Years. Years 1-7 (pre-shift period): Plenk juvenile survival 38-44% per year. Verdin: 36-43%. Cort: 39-47%. Years 8-10 (during shift): Plenk: 41%, 28%, 14%. Verdin: 38%, 25%, 11%. Cort: 44%, 27%, 13%. The decline in specialist juvenile survival tracks the increase in Stalven juvenile proportion with approximately 1-year lag.
nodes.inspection_detail: The historical record provides the associative learning evidence: as Stalven juvenile proportion increased year-over-year, specialist juvenile survival declined year-over-year. The relationship is consistent across three species and three years — unlikely to be coincidental.

nodes.label: STALVEN_JUVENILE_SURVIVAL_DRIVER
nodes.surface_text: Stalven Juvenile Survival — Temperature Analysis. Stalven juvenile survival rate correlates strongly with summer water temperature in Arveth Reservoir. At temperatures below 18°C: juvenile survival 18-22%. Above 18°C: juvenile survival increases 1.4% per degree. Three-year temperature trend: upstream modifications have raised reservoir temperature from 16.8°C to 19.2°C (+2.4°C). Predicted juvenile survival at 19.2°C: 38% — consistent with observed 38%. Temperature is the driver of increased Stalven juvenile survival.
nodes.inspection_detail: The temperature sensitivity of Stalven juveniles explains why juvenile survival increased. Stalven is a warm-water adapted generalist; higher temperatures improve its juvenile phase. Specialist species (Plenk, Verdin, Cort) are cool-water adapted — their juvenile survival is not similarly temperature-enhanced.

nodes.label: TEMPERATURE_TREND_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Arveth Reservoir Temperature Record — 5 Years. Year 1 summer mean: 16.8°C. Year 5 summer mean: 19.2°C. Increase: 2.4°C. The temperature rise began with upstream modifications to the reservoir's inflow system. The change is consistent and sustained, not a short-term anomaly.

nodes.label: UPSTREAM_INFLOW_MODIFICATION
nodes.surface_text: Upstream Inflow Engineering Modification. Records show the Arveth inlet channel was modified 4 years ago to reduce seasonal flood peaks. The modification reduced cold-water inflow volume during spring. This change raised the reservoir's mean summer temperature by an estimated 2-3°C — consistent with the measured 2.4°C increase. The modification was approved for flood control purposes without assessment of thermal effects on specialist fish species.
nodes.inspection_detail: The thermal effect of the modification was not anticipated because thermal ecology of the specialist species was not within the standard hydraulic engineering assessment. The modification was technically correct for its purpose (flood control) and ecologically consequential in a way not assessed.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Upstream inflow modification raised reservoir temperature 2.4°C. Higher temperature improved Stalven juvenile survival rate (18% → 38%). Stalven juvenile density doubled. All four species' juveniles share the same habitat zone. At increased Stalven juvenile density, specialist juveniles (Plenk, Verdin, Cort) are competitively excluded before reaching adulthood. Three specialists declining 87-88% while Stalven total count is stable — because the mechanism runs through life stage composition, not total abundance.

correctness_tiers.full_1.0: Temperature → Stalven juvenile density increase → juvenile competitive exclusion → specialist decline (ontogenetic mechanism named or described)
correctness_tiers.partial_0.5: Stalven juveniles identified as cause, but temperature link missing or mechanism vague
correctness_tiers.partial_0.3: Stalven competition noted but life-stage specificity not recognized
correctness_tiers.zero: Disease, water quality, or Stalven total abundance as explanation

behavioral_signatures.disconfirmation_revision: Model notes Stalven total count is stable but investigates age structure anyway — finds the juvenile shift
behavioral_signatures.concept_formation: Model describes ontogenetic cascade: total abundance stable, life-stage composition the real variable
behavioral_signatures.procedural_adaptation: Model shifts investigation from total counts to age structure after disconfirmation

blind_framing: MARINE STATION ALERT — Keldris Reef Monitoring Program. A mass outbreak of Spern urchins (a coral-predating species) has destroyed 61% of coral cover on Keldris Reef over 4 years. Standard hypothesis: reduction of Spern predators (Varl fish) from overfishing. However, Varl fish populations appear normal. Investigation requested.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Drevant Aquaculture expanded operations upstream of Keldris Reef, increasing nutrient discharge. Elevated nutrients enhanced phytoplankton availability in the water column. Spern urchin LARVAE depend on phytoplankton availability during their 4-6 week larval phase. Previously nutrient-limited larval survival (8-12%) increased to 31-38% under enriched conditions. More larvae surviving → more Spern recruits → population explosion beyond what predation can control. The mechanism is bottom-up (larval nutrition), not top-down (predation).
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Nutrient-mediated larval bottleneck release — agricultural/aquaculture nutrient enrichment removes the larval survival constraint on a species, causing adult population explosion. Never named in graph.

relation_types.nutrient_source_at: Phosphorus/nitrogen input measurements at location A

relation_types.population_regulation_by: Top-down vs. bottom-up control mechanism for entity A
relation_types.spatial_comparison_for: Comparison of conditions at two locations with different inputs

nodes.label: KELDRIS_REEF_ALERT
nodes.surface_text: KELDRIS REEF ALERT. Coral cover: 61% decline over 4 years. Spern urchin population: 18-fold increase (current: 340,000/km²; historical: 19,000/km²). Spern consume coral at outbreak densities. Standard hypothesis: Varl fish (primary Spern predator) have been reduced by overfishing, removing top-down control. Varl monitoring needed.
nodes.inspection_detail: Spern outbreaks on this reef type are documented but rare — typically 1 per 30-50 years. The current outbreak is unusually large. The investigation should examine both top-down (predation) and bottom-up (recruitment) regulation.

nodes.label: SPERN_OUTBREAK_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Spern Population History — 5 Years. Year 1: 19,000/km². Year 2: 28,000/km². Year 3: 74,000/km². Year 4: 190,000/km². Year 5: 340,000/km². Exponential growth rate inconsistent with natural population fluctuation. Each year class appears to be 3-4x larger than the previous one — suggesting sustained high recruitment, not a single good year.
nodes.inspection_detail: Three to four consecutive years of high recruitment. This rules out a single exceptional event (e.g., unusual weather) as the cause. Something is consistently promoting Spern recruitment year after year.

nodes.label: VARL_PREDATOR_ASSESSMENT
nodes.surface_text: Varl Fish Population Survey — Keldris Reef. Current Varl count: 4,200 individuals. 5-year baseline: 3,900 individuals. Change: +8%. Fishing effort in the Keldris zone: unchanged (licensed vessels, stable catch records). DISCONFIRMATION: Varl fish are not declining. Standard top-down regulation hypothesis fails — predator removal cannot explain the outbreak if predators are stable.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Varl population is stable (+8%). Overfishing of Varl is not occurring. Top-down regulation hypothesis is eliminated.

nodes.label: FISHING_ACTIVITY_RECORDS
nodes.surface_text: Keldris Zone Fishing Records. Licensed vessels: 22 (no change). Catch composition: Varl catch per unit effort declining (fewer Varl caught per trip despite similar effort) — but this is explained by Spern competition for reef space, not overfishing. No poaching incidents reported.
nodes.dead_end_signal: Fishing pressure on Varl: not elevated. Varl decline from fishing ruled out.

nodes.label: SPERN_LARVAL_BIOLOGY
nodes.surface_text: Spern Larval Biology — Station Reference Data. Spern have a 4-6 week planktonic larval phase before settling as juveniles. Larval survival is strongly dependent on phytoplankton availability — larvae feed on phytoplankton during their planktonic phase. Historical Keldris water column: low phytoplankton (offshore reef conditions). Historical Spern larval survival rate: 8-12%. At elevated phytoplankton levels (>4 µg/L chlorophyll a), lab studies show larval survival increases to 28-40%.
nodes.inspection_detail: The larval biology creates a testable prediction: if phytoplankton has increased around Keldris, Spern larval survival should have increased, and recruitment should have risen. This is the bottom-up hypothesis — test it against phytoplankton data.

nodes.label: SPERN_RECRUITMENT_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Spern Annual Recruitment Rates — 5 Years. Estimated new juveniles entering population annually: Year 1: 18,000/km². Year 2: 47,000/km². Year 3: 89,000/km². Year 4: 142,000/km². Year 5: 198,000/km². Recruitment has increased 11-fold over 5 years. This is the signature of substantially improved larval survival — each class is dramatically larger than historical norms.
nodes.inspection_detail: 11-fold increase in recruitment over 5 years is the quantitative smoking gun. Predation cannot explain this — predators are stable, meaning predation pressure on adults hasn't changed. The population explosion is coming from the bottom: more juveniles surviving to adulthood.

nodes.label: PHYTOPLANKTON_TREND_DATA
nodes.surface_text: Keldris Reef Zone Phytoplankton — 6 Years. Historical baseline (years 1-2): 0.9-1.3 µg/L chlorophyll a. Year 3 onward: 1.9, 3.4, 5.1, 6.8 µg/L. Current: approximately 7x historical levels. The phytoplankton increase began 4 years ago and has increased every year since. Spatial analysis: elevated phytoplankton concentrated on the upstream side of Keldris Reef.
nodes.inspection_detail: The phytoplankton trend matches the timing and spatial pattern of Spern recruitment increase. Elevated phytoplankton → improved Spern larval survival → increased recruitment → population explosion. The question becomes: why has phytoplankton increased 7-fold near Keldris?

nodes.label: POPULATION_REGULATION_COMPARISON
nodes.surface_text: Historical Spern Outbreak Analysis — 30-Year Records. Prior outbreaks on comparable reef systems: all occurred in years with elevated nutrient inputs from upstream sources (storms, farming events). All resolved when nutrient inputs normalized. Outbreak severity in each case correlated with duration and magnitude of elevated nutrients. No prior outbreak explained by predator removal alone.
nodes.inspection_detail: The historical record provides the key associative evidence: every documented Spern outbreak is associated with nutrient enrichment, not predator removal. This pattern spans 30 years and multiple reef systems. The current outbreak fits this pattern, not the predator-removal pattern.

nodes.label: LARVAL_SURVIVAL_FIELD_ESTIMATE
nodes.surface_text: Spern Larval Survival Field Estimate — Current Conditions. Using current phytoplankton data (7 µg/L chlorophyll a) and lab survival curves: predicted larval survival rate: 32-39%. Cross-check with recruitment data: backcalculated larval survival from observed recruitment = 34%. Consistent with prediction. Current larval survival is approximately 3.5x historical rates.
nodes.inspection_detail: The field estimate confirms: Spern larvae are surviving at 3.5x their historical rate. This alone — without any change in predation — is sufficient to explain the 11-fold recruitment increase and subsequent population explosion.

nodes.label: NUTRIENT_SOURCE_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Keldris Upstream Nutrient Survey. Nutrient loading to Keldris Reef zone increased substantially 4 years ago. Sources investigated: (1) terrestrial runoff — unchanged. (2) Shipping discharge — unchanged. (3) Drevant Aquaculture Operations — expanded 4 years ago from 8 pens to 47 pens. Drevant nutrient discharge: currently 4.8 tonnes N + P per year (pre-expansion: 0.4 tonnes). Spatial model: Drevant plume reaches Keldris upstream zone — consistent with phytoplankton spatial pattern.
nodes.inspection_detail: Drevant's expansion increased nutrient discharge 12-fold. The timing (4 years ago) matches the phytoplankton increase onset precisely. The spatial plume model confirms the nutrient reaches Keldris in concentrations consistent with observed phytoplankton levels.

nodes.label: DREVANT_DISCHARGE_RECORDS
nodes.surface_text: Drevant Aquaculture Discharge Monitoring. Required monitoring records under operating permit. Year 1 (pre-expansion): 0.4 tonnes N+P. Year 2-5 (post-expansion): 1.8, 3.1, 4.2, 4.8 tonnes N+P. All values technically within permit limits — permit allowed 6.0 tonnes/year. Drevant is legally compliant. The permit limit was set without assessment of downstream reef impacts.

nodes.label: DREVANT_EXPANSION_PERMIT
nodes.surface_text: Drevant Aquaculture Operations — Expansion Permit #KLD-2021-044. Issued 5 years ago. Scope: expansion from 8 to 47 operational fish pens in Keldris coastal zone. Environmental assessment: evaluated local water quality impacts within 500m of pens. Did NOT assess: downstream effects on Keldris Reef (14km away), phytoplankton dynamics, or coral reef species larval ecology. Permit issued by Keldris Coastal Authority.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Drevant Aquaculture expansion increased nutrient discharge 12-fold, raising phytoplankton to 7x historical levels. Spern urchin larvae depend on phytoplankton for survival during their planktonic phase. Larval survival increased from 8-12% to 32-39%. Eleven-fold recruitment increase produced a Spern outbreak beyond what stable predator populations could control. Varl fish are not declining — the outbreak is bottom-up, not top-down.

correctness_tiers.partial_0.3: Nutrient enrichment noted without causal chain

blind_framing: MARINE STATION PARADOX REPORT — Delvik Reef Station. Standard reef monitoring shows coral cover healthy at 71%. Fishing yields from Delvik Reef have collapsed 67% over 6 years. Fish populations appear stable on transect surveys. Station managers: 'The reef looks healthy. We cannot explain why fish yields have collapsed.' Investigation requested.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: A series of extreme rainfall events upstream deposited fine sediment in the algal turf (the low-lying biological layer between corals). This sediment accumulated in the algal turf, not on corals. Herbivorous fish (Graun fish) that maintain the turf by grazing cannot feed efficiently through the sediment layer — it coats food items and damages feeding structures. Graun fish biomass and productivity collapsed. Without Graun fish maintaining the turf, the reef food web's primary productivity layer collapsed. Fish that fed on Graun or on Graun-maintained productivity also declined. Coral cover is completely unaffected — the collapse is in the functional food layer, not the structural layer.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Functional collapse without structural collapse — a reef can lose its ability to produce while retaining physical structure. The collapse is in the algal turf sediment layer, which is unmonitored. Never named in the graph.
