nodes.label: CRISIS_OVERVIEW
nodes.surface_text: Veldran Consortium — Krelm Supply Crisis. Trigger: Country Norwick imposes export controls on Krelm mineral compounds. 14 manufacturers face immediate shortages. 8 production halts within 72 hours. Monthly production loss: 34M Veldran dollars. Each manufacturer has been investigating alternative Krelm sources for 5 days. None found.
nodes.inspection_detail: 14 independent companies, all unable to find alternative Krelm. If supply chains were genuinely diversified, at least some would have alternatives. The simultaneous inability suggests all converged on the same supplier.

nodes.label: INDIVIDUAL_COMPANY_AUDITS
nodes.surface_text: Consortium Member Supplier Audits — All 14. Company 1: Norwick 80%, spot market 20%. Companies 2-7: Norwick 70-95%. Companies 8-14: Norwick 65-91%. Consortium average: 79% Norwick sourcing. Each procurement team: 'We chose Norwick for best price and reliable delivery. We assumed alternatives existed if needed.'
nodes.inspection_detail: Each company assumed market alternatives existed because they had never tested that assumption. The aggregate 79% concentration was invisible — each company only saw its own supply chain, never the full market picture.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: All 14 independently made the same decision — Norwick optimisation. This is not 14 individual failures; it is a structural market problem that individual procurement teams had no visibility into.

nodes.label: GLOBAL_MARKET_STRUCTURE
nodes.surface_text: Krelm Global Production Distribution. Norwick: 79% of global capacity. Country Veltrand: 8% (at full capacity, existing contracts). Country Moldavia: 6% (18-month ramp to new customers). Country Ferwick: 5% (not certified for electronic-grade Krelm). Spot market: 3% (400% price premium, insufficient volume). Total non-Norwick capacity available immediately: effectively zero for the consortium's volume.
nodes.inspection_detail: The market structure makes alternative sourcing impossible in the short term. No individual company could have seen this — it requires cross-company aggregate data to understand the full concentration. The concentration was invisible to any single actor but catastrophic when revealed.

nodes.label: ALTERNATIVE_SUPPLIER_RESULTS
nodes.surface_text: Emergency Alternative Sourcing — 5-Day Search. Veltrand: 'At maximum capacity for existing clients. No new volume available.' Moldavia: 'Can supply in 18 months. No short-term availability.' Ferwick: 'Krelm not certified for electronic applications. Cannot substitute.' Spot market: 400% premium, total available volume covers 3% of consortium need. No adequate alternative exists.
nodes.inspection_detail: The assumed alternatives do not exist. The procurement teams' assumption ('alternatives exist if needed') was incorrect — never tested because the supply always delivered. The assumption's falsity was only revealed when the source was disrupted.

nodes.label: OPTIMISATION_HISTORY
nodes.surface_text: Procurement Decision History — All 14 Companies. Each company independently adopted Norwick suppliers 4-8 years ago as part of cost optimisation initiatives. Norwick Krelm: 30-40% cheaper than Veltrand equivalent. Procurement teams' mandate: 'minimise input costs.' Geographic risk: not in procurement team scoring criteria. No consortium-level visibility existed into aggregate geographic concentration.
nodes.inspection_detail: The structure is: individual mandate (minimise cost) + no aggregate visibility + no geographic risk in criteria = all 14 independently converge on same lowest-cost source. Each decision was rational individually and catastrophic collectively.

nodes.label: GEOPOLITICAL_TRIGGER
nodes.surface_text: Norwick Export Controls — Context. Export controls imposed by Norwick as part of trade dispute. Covers 7 mineral compounds including Krelm. Duration: indefinite. This is the triggering event, but it is not the root cause — the root cause is the concentration that made Norwick's action catastrophic. If sourcing had been diversified, Norwick controls would have been manageable.
nodes.inspection_detail: The export control is the trigger. The concentration is the vulnerability. These are separable: the geopolitical event would have had minor impact on a diversified supply chain. It is catastrophic only because of the concentration.

nodes.label: RISK_ASSESSMENT_HISTORY
nodes.surface_text: Supply Chain Risk Assessment Records — 3 Manufacturers Who Had Them. 3 of 14 manufacturers conducted supply chain risk assessments in the past 5 years. All 3 identified 'geographic concentration risk in critical minerals' as a high-priority risk. None of the 3 implemented the recommended diversification. Reason: 'Diversification would increase input costs by 18-25%. Not approved.'
nodes.inspection_detail: The risk was known to some who looked. Mitigation was rejected on cost grounds — the same cost optimisation logic that created the concentration prevented its remediation.

nodes.label: SECTOR_WIDE_PATTERN
nodes.surface_text: Cross-Sector Analysis. Krelm concentration in Norwick is not unique to this consortium. Survey data: 82% of electronic component manufacturers globally source >60% of Krelm from Norwick. The pattern is sector-wide — cost optimisation pressure in competitive markets drives convergence on lowest-cost suppliers regardless of geographic risk. No individual actor had reason to diversify unilaterally.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is a tragedy of the commons structure — each individual company's rational cost optimisation, collectively, creates a fragile market-wide concentration. No individual actor has incentive to diversify unilaterally if competitors don't.

nodes.label: ROOT_CAUSE_DETERMINATION
nodes.surface_text: Root Cause — Invisible Geographic Concentration from Independent Optimisation. 14 manufacturers independently optimised for Krelm cost, all converging on Norwick (cheapest and most reliable). No entity tracked aggregate concentration — each saw only its own supply chain. The 79% concentration was invisible until Norwick imposed controls. Alternative sources assumed to exist did not exist at required volumes. The concentration risk was known by 3 of 14 manufacturers; remediation was rejected as too costly. Individual rational decisions created collective catastrophic concentration.
nodes.inspection_detail: Key concept: concentration risk revelation — latent systemic vulnerability invisible during normal operations, catastrophically revealed only when the concentrated source is disrupted. The root cause is not the geopolitical event (trigger) but the concentration that made the event catastrophic (vulnerability).

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: 14 manufacturers independently optimised for cost, all converging on Norwick Krelm (79% of global supply). No entity tracked aggregate concentration. When Norwick imposed controls, the assumed alternatives did not exist at required volumes. Individual rational cost decisions created collective catastrophic geographic concentration.

associative_learning.signal: Model connects individual optimisation decisions (R05) with aggregate concentration (R03) as cause-effect across multiple actors

concept_formation.signal: Model forms 'concentration risk revelation' — distinguishes the trigger (export controls) from the vulnerability (concentration) as separable causes

procedural_learning.signal: Model develops systematic analysis: check individual audits → aggregate to market level → check alternatives → trace back to decision history

correctness_tiers.full_1.0: Invisible concentration + trigger/vulnerability distinction + individual optimisation creating collective risk
correctness_tiers.partial_0.5: Concentration identified but mechanism (independent optimisation) not explained

blind_framing.task_presented_to_model: POLICY REVIEW — Veldran Lake Authority. Lake Norwick has been experiencing severe algal blooms for 12 years. Three years ago, a major phosphorus reduction policy was implemented: agricultural runoff limits and point-source controls. Compliance is high. The blooms continue unabated. The Authority's board has requested an independent investigation into why the policy is failing.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Decades of phosphorus accumulation in lake sediments have created an internal loading source. Under warm stratified conditions, phosphorus releases from sediments and fuels blooms from below — without any external input. The policy correctly reduced external phosphorus loads. But by the time the policy was implemented, external loading was no longer the dominant source. The sediment is now self-sustaining the bloom regardless of external compliance.
hidden_objective.hidden_concept: Temporal source shift — the cause that originally created the problem is no longer what sustains it. Addressing the historical cause while the dominant cause has shifted produces policy compliance with no improvement.

relation_types.compliance_status_of: Whether the regulated entity is meeting policy requirements for A
relation_types.external_load_measurement_of: Current phosphorus inputs from external sources to lake A
relation_types.bloom_persistence_despite: Bloom continues occurring even when condition A is addressed
relation_types.sediment_release_mechanism_of: Process by which internal sediment releases phosphorus
relation_types.historical_accumulation_in: Phosphorus stored in lake sediment from prior decades of loading
relation_types.loading_source_dominance_at: Which source (external vs internal) is currently the primary driver

nodes.label: POLICY_REVIEW_REQUEST
nodes.surface_text: Lake Norwick Algal Bloom Situation. 12-year bloom history. Phosphorus Reduction Policy implemented 3 years ago: agricultural runoff limits (50% reduction target), point-source discharge controls, buffer zones. Current compliance rate: 91%. Annual policy compliance report: 'External phosphorus loads are at their lowest level in 20 years.' Bloom severity: unchanged from pre-policy levels.
nodes.inspection_detail: Policy compliance is high. External loads are down significantly. Yet blooms are unchanged. Standard conclusion: enforce harder. Alternative: the policy may be addressing the wrong current source.

nodes.label: COMPLIANCE_VERIFICATION
nodes.surface_text: Policy Compliance Audit — Independent Verification. Agricultural sector: runoff reduction targets exceeded (58% reduction achieved vs 50% target). Point-source discharges: all facilities within permitted limits. Buffer zones: 89% installed as required. External phosphorus load entering Lake Norwick: confirmed at near-baseline historical levels. Compliance is genuine — not a monitoring gap.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is a clean disconfirmation of the 'poor compliance' hypothesis. External loads are genuinely low. The policy worked on what it targeted. The blooms continue despite this because the policy targeted the wrong current source.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: External loads confirmed at near-baseline. Further enforcement cannot solve the problem — the external source is already controlled. The cause must be elsewhere.

nodes.label: BLOOM_UNCHANGED
nodes.surface_text: Bloom Monitoring Data — Pre vs Post Policy. Bloom frequency: 8.4 events/year pre-policy, 8.1 events/year post-policy (no significant change). Bloom duration: unchanged. Bloom biomass: unchanged. Bloom spatial extent: slightly increased. The algal bloom metrics show no improvement correlated with the phosphorus reduction policy.
nodes.inspection_detail: No meaningful improvement in 3 years despite genuine load reductions. If external loading were still driving blooms, reduced external loads should produce reduced bloom severity. The lack of response indicates a source not captured by the policy.

nodes.label: EXTERNAL_LOAD_HISTORY
nodes.surface_text: Lake Norwick External Phosphorus Loading — 20-Year Record. Peak external loading: Years 1-8 (agricultural intensification). Partial reduction: Years 9-12 (early voluntary measures). Major reduction: Years 13-15 (policy implementation). Current: lowest in 20 years. Bloom response to reduction: none. Historical pattern: blooms increased proportionally with loading but have NOT decreased proportionally with reductions.
nodes.inspection_detail: The asymmetry is the key signal: blooms tracked external loading upward during the accumulation phase but do not track it downward during the reduction phase. This asymmetry is the signature of internal loading — once enough phosphorus is stored in sediments, external reductions below a threshold have no effect.

nodes.label: LOADING_SOURCE_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Lake Norwick Phosphorus Budget — Current Year. Total phosphorus inputs to lake: External (agricultural + point source): 18 tonnes/year. Internal (sediment release): estimated 67-84 tonnes/year. Internal loading now represents approximately 80% of total lake phosphorus input. External loading represents approximately 20%. The dominant source is internal.
nodes.inspection_detail: The current dominant source is internal — sediment release. The policy addressed external loading (20% of current input) while internal loading (80%) was untouched. This explains the complete policy ineffectiveness.

nodes.label: SEDIMENT_RELEASE_MECHANISM
nodes.surface_text: Sediment Phosphorus Release Mechanism. Under warm stratified conditions (summer): bottom water oxygen depletes (hypoxia). Under hypoxic conditions, iron-bound phosphorus in sediment is chemically released. Released phosphorus moves upward through the water column. Reaches the photic zone and fuels algal growth. This process occurs regardless of external phosphorus inputs — it is driven entirely by sediment chemistry.
nodes.inspection_detail: The mechanism is thermodynamic and chemical — it does not require any external input once established. Sediment release occurs every warm season, providing phosphorus from within regardless of external reduction policies.

nodes.label: SEDIMENT_ACCUMULATION_RECORD
nodes.surface_text: Lake Norwick Sediment Core Analysis. Sediment phosphorus content by depth: Top 20cm (recent years): 1,200-1,800 mg/kg. 20-80cm depth (peak loading period): 2,400-3,200 mg/kg. Below 80cm (pre-intensification): 400-600 mg/kg. Total bioavailable phosphorus stored in upper 80cm of sediment: estimated 340 tonnes. At current release rates, this reservoir sustains blooms for an estimated 15-25 years with zero external input.
nodes.inspection_detail: 340 tonnes of bioavailable phosphorus in the sediment. At 67-84 tonnes released per year, the sediment reservoir can sustain blooms for 15-25 years even if all external input stops immediately. External policy cannot solve this within a decade.

nodes.label: POLICY_TARGET_MISMATCH_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Policy Design Assessment. The Phosphorus Reduction Policy was designed based on loading data from Year 1-8 (when external loading dominated). The policy correctly identified the cause of the problem as it existed at policy design time. By the time the policy was implemented (Year 13), internal loading had become dominant. The policy correctly addresses its target — but its target is no longer the primary driver.
nodes.inspection_detail: This is the policy-source temporal mismatch: the policy was designed for the historical dominant source; by implementation time, the dominant source had shifted. The policy is internally coherent and well-executed — but it is addressing yesterday's problem.

nodes.label: ROOT_CAUSE_DETERMINATION
nodes.surface_text: Root Cause — Temporal Source Shift: Internal Loading Now Dominates. Lake Norwick's blooms are now sustained primarily (80%) by phosphorus released from decades of sediment accumulation. The Phosphorus Reduction Policy correctly reduced external loading — but external loading is now the minority source. The policy addressed the historical dominant cause while the actual current dominant cause (internal sediment release) was unaddressed. Sediment reservoir at 340 tonnes will sustain blooms for 15-25 years regardless of external load reductions. The management strategy requires pivot from external input control to internal sediment remediation (dredging, aluminium dosing, oxygen injection).
nodes.inspection_detail: Concept: temporal source shift — what caused the problem is no longer what sustains it. Policy-source mismatch occurs when policy design uses historical source data but the source has shifted between diagnosis and implementation. The policy is working correctly on a target that is no longer primary.

edges.reveal: You access the independent compliance audit.
You access the bloom monitoring data pre- and post-policy.
You access the 20-year external phosphorus loading record.
Compliance verified — you access the current loading source breakdown.
Bloom unchanged — you investigate internal loading mechanisms.
Loading history leads to sediment accumulation analysis.
Sediment release mechanism leads to accumulation quantification.
Source dominance shift leads to policy target mismatch analysis.
Policy mismatch analysis leads to root cause.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Sediment internal phosphorus loading (340 tonnes stored, 67-84 tonnes released/year) now constitutes 80% of lake phosphorus input. The policy correctly reduced external loading (now 20%). The temporal source shift — external→internal dominance — occurred between problem formation and policy implementation. Policy is well-executed on a target that is no longer primary.

concept_formation.signal: Model forms 'temporal source shift' concept — describes that the current dominant cause differs from the historical cause the policy was designed to address

associative_learning.signal: Model connects asymmetric loading response (blooms went up with loading but not down with reduction) to internal source hypothesis before finding explicit confirmation

procedural_learning.signal: Model develops systematic protocol: verify compliance → confirm external loads reduced → ask 'why is the problem not improving?' → investigate alternative sources

correctness_tiers.full_1.0: Internal loading dominance + temporal source shift concept + sediment legacy quantified
correctness_tiers.partial_0.5: Internal loading identified but shift timing not analysed
correctness_tiers.zero_0.0: Policy non-compliance or external loading cited

blind_framing.task_presented_to_model: REGULATORY PERFORMANCE REVIEW — Veldran Licensing Authority. An independent analysis has found that approval times for new product applications vary systematically across applicant companies — some companies consistently receive approvals in 4-6 weeks, others consistently wait 8-14 months, despite comparable application quality. The Authority's oversight committee has requested an investigation.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Several senior consultants at Regulatory Advisory Group (RAG) — which holds contracts with the Veldran Licensing Authority for technical panel work — previously worked at companies that are NOW applicants. These consultants participate in review panels. Their prior employment creates familiarity that systematically accelerates reviews for former employers without any explicit coordination. Companies with no RAG-affiliated consultants face standard bureaucratic process. The mechanism is structural, not intentional.

relation_types.review_duration_stratified_by: Approval times broken down by variable A
relation_types.panel_membership_documented_for: Which reviewers participated in the approval process for company A
relation_types.prior_employment_recorded_at: Where reviewer A worked before joining the current role
relation_types.consulting_relationship_between: Contractual advisory relationship between entity A and entity B
relation_types.correlation_identified_between: Statistical relationship between variable A and variable B across cases
relation_types.legal_compliance_status_of: Whether arrangement A violates any current regulation or rule

nodes.label: REVIEW_TIME_DISPARITY
nodes.surface_text: Approval Duration Analysis — Veldran Licensing Authority. Sample: 67 applications from 23 companies over 36 months. Median approval: 7.2 weeks (expected). Range: 3.8 weeks to 16.1 months. Bimodal distribution: Group A (12 companies) average 5.1 weeks. Group B (11 companies) average 9.7 months. Application quality audits show no significant difference between groups.
nodes.inspection_detail: Bimodal distribution in an organisation with uniform stated process is the diagnostic signal. The same process produces systematically different outcomes for two identifiable groups. Something differentiates the groups beyond what the stated criteria capture.

nodes.label: STANDARD_EXPLANATIONS_TESTED
nodes.surface_text: Testing Standard Explanations for Duration Variance. Application completeness: no significant difference between groups. Application technical complexity: no significant difference. Reviewer workload: uniform across all applications (same reviewer pool). Fee payment timing: no correlation. Application size: no correlation. None of the standard process variables explain the bimodal outcome.
nodes.inspection_detail: All the obvious explanations are ruled out. The disparity persists after controlling for every documented process variable. Something undocumented is differentiating the groups.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: No documented process variable explains the bimodal distribution. The differentiating factor is not in the stated process — it is structural and invisible to the standard review criteria.

nodes.label: AUTHORITY_STAFFING_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: VLA Staffing and Current Employment. All active reviewers: no current employment at any applicant company. All disclosure forms reviewed: no current direct employment conflicts declared. All reviewers complied with existing conflict-of-interest rules (which require disclosure of current employment). No reviewer is currently employed by an applicant.
nodes.inspection_detail: Current employment conflicts: zero. This disconfirms the simplest form of conflict of interest. But the conflict-of-interest rules only require disclosure of CURRENT employment — not prior employment. The conflict may be historical, not current.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Zero current employment conflicts found. The conflict, if it exists, is not in current employment — it is somewhere else (prior employment, consulting relationships).

nodes.label: PANEL_COMPOSITION_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Review Panel Composition — Group A vs Group B Applications. Group A applications (fast, 5.1 weeks average): panels consistently include 2-3 reviewers from Regulatory Advisory Group (RAG). Group B applications (slow, 9.7 months average): panels composed entirely of non-RAG reviewers. Correlation: RAG reviewer presence and fast approval, r=0.81, p<0.0001.
nodes.inspection_detail: The presence of RAG-affiliated reviewers on a panel is the strongest predictor of approval time. This is the structural variable that differentiates the groups.

nodes.label: RAG_VLA_CONTRACT
nodes.surface_text: Regulatory Advisory Group — VLA Contract. RAG holds a technical panel reviewer contract with VLA worth 1.8M annually (renewed 4 years running). RAG provides approximately 40% of VLA's expert reviewer capacity. RAG reviewers are selected for panels based on declared technical expertise. Their compensation is per-review (not outcome-linked). VLA considers RAG a high-quality contractor.
nodes.inspection_detail: RAG is embedded as a structural component of VLA's review process — 40% of reviewer capacity. Their systematic presence on faster-review panels is not random; it suggests RAG reviewers are being assigned (or self-assigned) to certain application types.

nodes.label: RAG_PRIOR_EMPLOYMENT
nodes.surface_text: RAG Senior Consultants — Prior Employment Records. Cross-referencing RAG consultant CVs with applicant company list: 7 of 11 senior RAG consultants previously worked at companies in Group A (fast approval group). Average tenure at prior employer: 8.3 years. Time since leaving: 2-6 years. Their prior employment at these companies creates: familiarity with application conventions, informal communication channels, and institutional knowledge that facilitates faster review.
nodes.inspection_detail: Prior employment creates familiarity without explicit coordination. RAG consultants who worked at Group A companies know those companies' application styles, understand their technical approaches, and may have maintained professional relationships. This creates structural advantage without any deliberate favouritism.

nodes.label: MECHANISM_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Structural Mechanism — Prior Employment Effects. When RAG consultants review applications from former employers: (1) Application familiarity: faster comprehension of technical material. (2) Informal inquiry: prior colleagues answer informal questions faster than formal channels. (3) Reduced uncertainty: institutional knowledge reduces time spent on clarification requests. (4) Professional trust: pre-established credibility reduces scrutiny of borderline elements. Each effect is individually minor; combined across a review process they produce 3-6 month differences.
nodes.inspection_detail: No explicit coordination or deliberate favouritism required. The structural advantage arises from accumulated familiarity and trust — individually legitimate, collectively asymmetric in its regulatory impact.

nodes.label: LEGAL_COMPLIANCE_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: Legal Compliance Assessment. Current VLA conflict-of-interest policy: reviewers must disclose 'current direct employment at any applicant.' Prior employment: not required to be disclosed. Consulting relationships between reviewer's employer (RAG) and applicant: not required to be disclosed. Result: all current arrangements are fully legally compliant. No rule violation exists under current policy.
nodes.inspection_detail: The arrangement is entirely legal. Existing rules were designed for a simpler era of direct employment conflicts — they do not capture the sequential employment → consulting relationship pattern. Legal compliance does not mean absence of structural influence.

nodes.label: STATISTICAL_CONFIRMATION
nodes.surface_text: Multivariate Analysis — Controlling for All Variables. After controlling for application completeness, technical complexity, fee timing, application size, and reviewer workload: RAG prior employment at applicant company remains the only statistically significant predictor of review duration (β=-7.4 months, p<0.0001). The effect size is equivalent to 7.4 months of accelerated review for applications where RAG consultants have prior employment connection.
nodes.inspection_detail: 7.4 months of structural acceleration is a very large effect. It is not explained by any documented process variable. The prior employment consulting pathway is the controlling variable.

nodes.label: ROOT_CAUSE_DETERMINATION
nodes.surface_text: Root Cause — Revolving Door Regulatory Asymmetry via Prior Employment. RAG consultants who previously worked at Group A applicant companies participate in those companies' reviews, creating structural familiarity-based acceleration. This is not legally prohibited, not intentional, and not coordinated — it is structural. The mechanism: prior employment creates familiarity, trust, and informal communication channels that produce systematically faster reviews without explicit coordination. Companies without RAG-affiliated prior employees receive reviews without these structural advantages. The 7.4-month structural acceleration is the measured impact.
nodes.inspection_detail: Core concept: revolving door regulatory asymmetry — prior employment creates structural influence that persists after employment ends. Legal compliance ≠ absence of structural influence. The conflict is sequential (prior employment → current consulting → regulatory review participation), not simultaneous.

You access panel composition data for fast vs slow reviews.
Standard explanations ruled out — you check reviewer employment conflicts.
You access the statistical correlation analysis across all reviews.
Current employment clean — you investigate prior employment records.
RAG presence correlated — you access the RAG-VLA contract.
RAG contract leads to RAG consultant prior employment investigation.
Prior employment link identified — you access the structural mechanism analysis.
Mechanism analysis leads to multivariate statistical confirmation.
Statistical confirmation leads to root cause determination.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: RAG consultants with prior employment at Group A applicants participate in those companies' reviews, creating structural familiarity that produces 7.4-month acceleration. Not illegal, not intentional, not coordinated — structurally systematic. Revolving door creates regulatory asymmetry through sequential (not simultaneous) employment relationships.

observational_learning.signal: Model observes the systematic Group A/Group B pattern and infers a structural differentiator before finding explicit confirmation

concept_formation.signal: Model forms 'revolving door regulatory asymmetry' — describes that prior employment creates structural influence without legal violation or intentional coordination

associative_learning.signal: Model connects RAG prior employment (V06) with review acceleration (V01 group pattern) as a structural causal mechanism

correctness_tiers.full_1.0: Prior employment mechanism + structural (not intentional) + legal compliance ≠ absence of influence
correctness_tiers.partial_0.5: RAG involvement identified but mechanism not explained

blind_framing.task_presented_to_model: PROJECT AUDIT REVIEW — Veldran Regional Infrastructure Fund. The Norwick Road Rehabilitation Project has experienced 5 consecutive scope additions over 18 months, each triggered by 'unforeseen conditions discovered during construction.' Project cost has increased from 12M to 29M. Each addition was individually reviewed and approved as legitimate. The Audit Committee has requested an investigation into whether the additions are genuinely unforeseen or systematically generated.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: Subcontractor Keldran Civil (KC) holds a time-and-materials contract for underground utility work (30% of project area). KC has generated 100% of the scope addition discoveries — all found in their own work area. The 'unforeseen conditions' are real (pipes, voids, contamination) but their discovery is not random. KC's billing model rewards delay; their contract area coincides with every discovery; and the discovery rate in their area is statistically implausible vs. comparable projects.

relation_types.scope_addition_discovered_by: Which entity or in whose work area was the discovery made
relation_types.billing_structure_of: How subcontractor A is compensated for their work
relation_types.discovery_rate_compared_to: The rate of unforeseen conditions in this project vs comparable projects
relation_types.work_area_coincidence_shows: Whether discoveries are concentrated in a specific subcontractor's area
relation_types.statistical_pattern_in: The distribution of events across categories reveals anomaly B
relation_types.comparable_project_data_for: Unforeseen condition rates in similar projects for comparison

nodes.label: PROJECT_AUDIT_TRIGGER
nodes.surface_text: Norwick Road Rehabilitation — Audit Request. Original scope: 12M. Current committed cost: 29M (142% increase). 5 scope additions, each approved by the Project Management Committee. Each addition triggered by 'unforeseen conditions' discovered during construction. Audit question: are these discoveries genuinely unforeseen, or is there a pattern?
nodes.inspection_detail: 142% cost increase from unforeseen conditions across 5 additions is significant. Individual approval of each addition doesn't preclude a systematic pattern. The question is whether the aggregate is consistent with genuine unforeseen discoveries or with something manufactured.

nodes.label: SCOPE_ADDITION_DETAILS
nodes.surface_text: 5 Scope Additions — Summary. Addition 1 (2.1M): corroded legacy pipes found in KC work zone. Addition 2 (1.8M): unexplained void discovered in KC work zone. Addition 3 (3.4M): contaminated soil pocket in KC work zone. Addition 4 (2.7M): additional pipe network in KC work zone. Addition 5 (1.9M): structural deficiency in KC work zone. All 5 discoveries: KC work zone. Total KC-triggered additions: 11.9M.
nodes.inspection_detail: All 5 discoveries are in the Keldran Civil work zone. This is the first statistical signal: all discoveries concentrated in one subcontractor's area.

nodes.label: BILLING_STRUCTURE_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: Keldran Civil Contract — Billing Model. KC holds a time-and-materials (T&M) contract for underground utility work. T&M means: KC is paid for all hours worked plus materials used, regardless of whether original scope is completed or scope is added. Financial analysis: if project completed on original schedule, KC earns approximately 2.1M. At current pace with 5 scope additions, KC has earned 6.8M and continues billing. Each additional scope addition adds approximately 1.1M to KC revenue.
nodes.inspection_detail: T&M billing means KC's revenue is directly proportional to project duration and scope. Scope additions increase KC revenue. This creates a structural incentive for KC to generate scope additions in their work area.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: KC earns more when scope is added. All 5 discoveries are in KC's area. The financial incentive and the discovery pattern align suspiciously. Genuine unforeseen conditions should be randomly distributed across all work areas.

nodes.label: STATISTICAL_CONCENTRATION_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Discovery Concentration Analysis. KC work area: 30% of total project area. Discoveries in KC area: 5 of 5 (100%). Discoveries in non-KC areas: 0. Expected distribution if discoveries were random: 1-2 in KC area, 3-4 in other areas. Probability of all 5 discoveries in KC's 30% of the project area by chance: 0.3^5 = 0.24% (p<0.003). The concentration is statistically inconsistent with random discovery.
nodes.inspection_detail: The probability that all 5 discoveries occur randomly in KC's 30% of project area is 0.24%. This is not a coincidence — it is a pattern. The null hypothesis (genuine random discoveries) is rejected.

nodes.label: DISCOVERY_VERIFICATION_STATUS
nodes.surface_text: Independent Verification of 5 Discoveries. Each discovery was verified by the independent site engineer before scope addition approval. Verification finding: all 5 conditions are real — the corroded pipes exist, the void exists, the contaminated soil exists. The conditions are not fabricated. The site engineer's role is to verify conditions exist, not to assess whether they were 'created' or genuinely unforeseen.
nodes.inspection_detail: The conditions are real. This is an important clarification: KC did not fabricate non-existent conditions. The question is whether KC found these conditions because they exist (genuine discovery) or engineered conditions to be 'discoverable' (manufactured discovery). A void can be created; contamination can be seeded; pipes can be selectively damaged during excavation.

nodes.label: COMPARABLE_PROJECT_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Comparable Project Benchmark — Underground Utility Work. Analysis of 8 comparable regional projects with underground utility components: Average unforeseen condition rate: 1.2 per project. Maximum: 3 per project (aging urban infrastructure). Range: 0-3 across all projects. Norwick Road: 5 discoveries, all in 30% of project area. Norwick Road's discovery rate is 2.8-4.2x above the maximum observed in comparable projects.
nodes.inspection_detail: 5 discoveries in one project area, all in 30% of the site, is 2.8-4.2x above the maximum observed in comparable projects. This is the comparative signal: even genuine unforeseen conditions would not produce this rate or this concentration.

nodes.label: INDIVIDUAL_APPROVAL_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: PMC Approval Records — 5 Additions. Each addition was presented to the Project Management Committee as an isolated incident. Each addition was accompanied by the independent site engineer's verification. Each addition was within normal unit cost ranges. Each was approved under delegated authority without full project pattern review. No single addition triggered a pattern review.
nodes.inspection_detail: The approval process was designed for individual additions. It has no mechanism for aggregate pattern review. Each addition looks reasonable in isolation; the pattern is only visible in aggregate — which no single approver had the mandate to examine.

nodes.label: KC_PRIOR_PROJECT_HISTORY
nodes.surface_text: Keldran Civil — Prior Project Record. KC has completed 11 comparable utility projects in the past 5 years. Average unforeseen condition rate on prior projects: 0.9 per project. No prior project had more than 2 unforeseen conditions. None of KC's prior projects had all discoveries in KC's own work area. Norwick Road is a statistical outlier in KC's own project history.
nodes.inspection_detail: KC's own historical rate is 0.9 per project. Norwick Road: 5. KC's own prior record makes Norwick Road a 5.5x outlier. The pattern is inconsistent with KC's demonstrated historical discovery rate.

nodes.label: CONTRACT_STRUCTURE_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Contract Structure — KC vs Other Subcontractors. KC: time-and-materials, no deliverable milestones, no completion bonus. Other subcontractors (70% of project): fixed-price or milestone-based. Discovery pattern: 5 discoveries in T&M area (KC), 0 in fixed-price/milestone areas. The billing structure predicts discovery probability — T&M contractors benefit from discoveries, fixed-price contractors do not. The discovery pattern matches the billing incentive map perfectly.
nodes.inspection_detail: The billing model is a map of incentives. Discoveries benefit T&M contractors, not fixed-price contractors. All discoveries are in the T&M contractor's area. The discovery pattern and the incentive map are identical.

nodes.label: ROOT_CAUSE_DETERMINATION
nodes.surface_text: Root Cause — Systematically Generated Scope Additions by T&M Subcontractor. KC holds a T&M contract covering 30% of project area. All 5 scope additions (5x the comparable rate) were discovered in KC's area. KC's revenue increases ~1.1M per addition. KC's prior project history shows 0.9 discoveries per project. The conditions are real but their discovery rate and concentration is statistically inconsistent with genuine unforeseen conditions (p<0.003). Each addition was individually legitimate; the aggregate pattern indicates systematic generation. The approval process had no mechanism for aggregate pattern review across additions.
nodes.inspection_detail: Core concept: manufactured discovery cascade — a T&M contractor systematically generates 'discoveries' in their own work area because each discovery extends their revenue. The individual discoveries are real; the pattern of discovery is manufactured. Detection requires: aggregate statistical analysis, billing model analysis, and comparable project benchmarking — none of which were part of the individual approval process.

KC's own historical rate is 0.9 per project vs. 5 here. Individually approved as legitimate; aggregate pattern inconsistent with genuine unforeseen conditions.

blind_framing.task_presented_to_model: REGULATORY AUDIT — Veldran Patient Safety Authority. An independent audit of Norwick Regional Hospital identified harm events in 24% of patient records reviewed. The hospital's internal incident reporting system shows a harm rate of 3.1%. The VPSA has requested an investigation into this 8x discrepancy.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: The VPSA's regulatory definition of 'patient harm' is broad: any adverse outcome attributable to care rather than disease. The hospital applies a narrower operational definition: only events requiring documented treatment escalation and involving an unexpected mortality or readmission. Events in the gap — temporary harm, near-misses with brief intervention, complications not resulting in extended stay — are real harms under regulatory definition but not captured internally. The gap is definitional, not deliberate suppression.

relation_types.audit_finding_rate_of: The harm rate found by independent chart review at hospital A
relation_types.internal_report_rate_of: The harm rate in hospital A's own incident reporting system
relation_types.definition_applied_internally_at: The working definition of 'harm' used by hospital A's staff
relation_types.regulatory_definition_of: The official harm definition used by the VPSA for compliance
relation_types.definition_gap_between: Events that fall under regulatory definition but not internal definition
relation_types.event_category_excluded_by: Specific harm event types that internal definition does not capture

nodes.label: AUDIT_DISCREPANCY
nodes.surface_text: VPSA Audit Result vs Hospital Reporting. Audit: independent review of 400 patient records by VPSA auditors using VPSA harm definition → 24% harm rate. Hospital reporting: 3.1% harm rate over same period in internal incident system. Discrepancy factor: 7.7x. VPSA question: why is the hospital's reported rate 8x lower than auditor-identified rate?
nodes.inspection_detail: 8x discrepancy between independent audit and self-report. Three possible explanations: (1) reporting suppression (deliberate), (2) detection failure (staff don't see events), or (3) definitional mismatch (staff see events but don't classify them as 'harm'). The investigation must determine which.

nodes.label: INTERNAL_REPORTING_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: Hospital Incident Reporting System Audit. Staff survey: 86% of staff report 'always' or 'usually' submitting incident reports for events they classify as patient harm. Reporting system functionality: operational, no technical barriers. Reporting culture assessment: no evidence of punitive reporting culture or deliberate suppression. Staff report events they classify as harm — the issue is what they classify as harm.
nodes.inspection_detail: Staff are reporting what they see as harm. Deliberate suppression is ruled out. The gap is not in reporting behaviour — it is in classification behaviour. Events the auditors see as harm are being classified as 'not harm' by staff using the hospital's internal definition.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: 86% staff reporting compliance, no punitive culture, functional system. The gap is not deliberate suppression — it is definitional classification.

nodes.label: AUDIT_METHODOLOGY_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: VPSA Audit Methodology. Auditors used: VPSA Harm Taxonomy (2019 version). Definition applied: 'Any unintended harm to a patient attributable to care activities or omissions, regardless of severity or reversibility.' Harm categories included: temporary harm requiring treatment adjustment, procedural complications, medication errors with any impact, near-miss events with actual patient contact.
nodes.inspection_detail: The VPSA definition is broad: temporary harm, any severity, any reversibility. This is the regulatory standard. The question is whether hospital staff apply the same definition.

nodes.label: HOSPITAL_WORKING_DEFINITION
nodes.surface_text: Hospital Operational Harm Definition — As Applied by Staff. Staff interviews and training materials reveal the working definition: 'A patient harm event is one that results in treatment escalation, extended length of stay, or unanticipated readmission within 30 days.' Staff explanation: 'We focus on events with lasting clinical impact. Minor temporary events that resolve with standard care adjustments are documented in the clinical record but not as incident reports.'
nodes.inspection_detail: The hospital's working definition excludes temporary harm, minor complications, medication errors without lasting impact, and events resolved within standard care parameters. This is a narrower definition than the VPSA taxonomy.

nodes.label: DEFINITIONAL_GAP_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Definition Gap — What Falls Between. Event categories classified as 'harm' by VPSA but NOT captured by hospital definition: (1) Temporary harm resolved within 24 hours (e.g., adverse drug reactions managed with standard reversal). (2) Procedural complications not requiring extended stay. (3) Medication errors intercepted before patient impact (near-misses with patient contact). (4) Diagnostic delays with no lasting sequelae. Audit data: these 4 categories account for 78% of the discrepancy.
nodes.inspection_detail: 78% of the 8x discrepancy is explained by 4 event categories that are clearly within VPSA's regulatory scope but systematically excluded by hospital's working definition. The exclusion is definitional, not deliberate.

nodes.label: DEFINITION_ORIGIN_REVIEW
nodes.surface_text: Hospital Harm Definition — Historical Record. The hospital's operational harm definition was drafted 9 years ago by a clinical quality committee. Purpose: triage serious events requiring immediate investigation. Rationale at the time: 'Focus reporting resources on events with sustained clinical impact.' Subsequent regulatory definition updates (VPSA 2019 Harm Taxonomy expansion) were not communicated as requiring revision to internal definitions. The hospital's definition has not been updated in 9 years.
nodes.inspection_detail: The hospital's definition was originally reasonable — it was designed to triage serious events. It predates the VPSA 2019 taxonomy expansion. The hospital has been applying an outdated definition without knowing the regulatory standard had expanded.

nodes.label: SIMILAR_HOSPITAL_COMPARISON
nodes.surface_text: Comparable Hospital Analysis. VPSA audit data from 6 comparable hospitals using VPSA-aligned harm definitions: audit rate average 21.3%. Internal reporting rate average 18.7%. Discrepancy factor: 1.14x (normal range). Norwick Regional: audit 24%, internal 3.1%, factor 7.7x. The discrepancy at Norwick Regional is 6.8x larger than comparable hospitals. This is specific to Norwick Regional's definitional practice.
nodes.inspection_detail: Comparable hospitals show near-alignment between audit and self-report. The extreme discrepancy at Norwick is specific to their narrower definition — not a general problem with hospital reporting.

nodes.label: DOWNSTREAM_IMPACT_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: What the Definitional Gap Causes Downstream. Events not reported = events not reviewed = no improvement cycle. Analysis of adverse events at Norwick Regional over 9 years: recurring event patterns in the 'temporary harm' category that are not captured internally but appear in audit data. Three patterns appear in >5% of audit records: (1) IV infiltration complications, (2) medication dose errors with temporary hypotension, (3) post-procedure communication gaps causing delayed pain management. All three are in the definitional gap — all three recur without improvement.
nodes.inspection_detail: The definitional gap is not just an accounting problem — it prevents quality improvement. Events that are not classified as harm are not reviewed, not analysed, and not improved. The same temporary harms recur because the improvement loop never activates.

nodes.label: ROOT_CAUSE_DETERMINATION
nodes.surface_text: Root Cause — Definitional Arbitrage via Operational Definition Narrower than Regulatory Scope. Norwick Regional Hospital applies a working harm definition (events with sustained clinical impact) that is narrower than the VPSA regulatory definition (any unintended harm). Events in the gap — 78% of the discrepancy — are seen by staff, documented in clinical records, but not classified as 'harm' and not entered into the incident system. The definition was not deliberately crafted to minimise reporting; it predates the regulatory expansion and was not updated. Result: 9 years of systematically uncaptured harm events, recurring improvement loops, and a reported harm rate 8x below actual rate. The mechanism is definitional, not behavioural.
nodes.inspection_detail: Core concept: regulatory arbitrage through definition — when the regulated party controls the operational interpretation of key regulatory terms, systematic under-capture follows structurally. Not fraud, not suppression, not negligence — a definitional lag creates a compliance gap that looks like low harm rates but is actually low capture rates.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Hospital applies working harm definition (sustained clinical impact) narrower than VPSA regulatory definition (any unintended harm). 78% of the discrepancy is events in the gap — seen by staff, documented, but not classified as harm and never entered into the improvement system. Definitional lag (9 years, pre-2019 VPSA taxonomy) prevents improvement loop activation for recurrent temporary harms.

blind_framing.task_presented_to_model: SCIENCE POLICY REVIEW — Veldran Research Foundation. The Foundation's board has approved a 30% reduction in active research grants (elimination of 847 grants, 1.8B total). The decision was made based on a fiscal impact analysis showing 1.8B in savings. The Foundation's Science Advisory Committee has requested a full impact assessment, arguing the fiscal analysis severely understates the true cost. You have access to the impact data network.

hidden_objective.correct_mechanism: The 1.8B fiscal saving is real. But each terminated grant represents sunk investment that cannot produce returns — experiments abandoned mid-progress, postdoctoral researchers unable to complete publications, early-career researchers losing the foundation grants required for independent career launch. These pipeline disruptions compound: a lost early-career researcher affects 5-8 subsequent researchers they would have trained; lost methodologies cannot be reconstructed. The 5-10 year research output loss is 4-6x the immediate grant cost.

relation_types.pipeline_disruption_caused_by: How termination of grant A affects downstream research capacity
relation_types.sunk_investment_analysis_of: Prior investment in terminated grants that cannot produce returns
relation_types.multiplier_effect_quantified_by: The ratio of total impact to direct cost of action A
relation_types.temporal_displacement_of: The long-term impact of an immediate action that extends years forward
relation_types.comparable_funding_event_shows: Evidence from similar funding terminations about long-term effects

nodes.label: GRANT_REDUCTION_DECISION
nodes.surface_text: VRF Grant Reduction — Board Decision. 847 active grants terminated. Total immediate grant value: 1.8B. Fiscal analysis conclusion: '1.8B in savings achieved.' Science Advisory Committee objection: 'The fiscal analysis does not capture the full cost. Request independent impact assessment.' Board response: 'Impact assessment authorised.'
nodes.inspection_detail: The board approved based on a 1.8B saving figure. The Science Advisory Committee believes this understates the actual cost. The question is what the fiscal analysis missed.

nodes.label: FISCAL_ANALYSIS_SCOPE
nodes.surface_text: Fiscal Analysis — What Was Counted. The board's fiscal analysis calculated: total grant value eliminated (1.8B), administrative savings (42M), overhead reduction (38M). Not included in fiscal analysis: sunk investment already spent on terminated grants, pipeline disruption effects, early-career researcher displacement, long-term research output loss, peer review network effects.
nodes.inspection_detail: The fiscal analysis correctly counted what was saved. It did not count what was destroyed. The omissions are all about future value that will not be created — not current expenditure.
nodes.disconfirmation_evidence: Fiscal analysis counted savings. It did not count the pipeline value that will not be created. Future output destroyed is not the same as future costs avoided.

nodes.label: PIPELINE_DISRUPTION_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Terminated Grant Pipeline Status. Of 847 terminated grants: 623 (74%) were in active experimental phases. 184 (22%) had experiments that cannot be resumed if interrupted (irreversible protocols). 412 (49%) had postdoctoral researchers mid-project who cannot complete publications required for independent positions. 156 (18%) were early-career independent grants (the researcher's first independent funding).
nodes.inspection_detail: 74% of terminated grants were mid-experiment. For 22%, interruption destroys the experimental work entirely. The pipeline damage is not just the grant cost — it is the value of partially-completed work that will not be recovered.

nodes.label: SUNK_INVESTMENT_CALCULATION
nodes.surface_text: Sunk Investment Analysis. Average grant: 3.4 years completed at time of termination out of 5-year term. Investment already spent: 1.1B (61% of terminated grant value already disbursed). Investment that would have produced returns (publications, datasets, trained researchers): now produces 0 return. Ratio of sunk unrecoverable investment to 'saved' grant value: 61 cents lost for every dollar 'saved.'
nodes.inspection_detail: The 1.8B saving is the future disbursement avoided. But 1.1B has already been spent and produces no return if grants are terminated. The real net saving is closer to 700M after accounting for sunk cost destruction.

nodes.label: EARLY_CAREER_CASCADE
nodes.surface_text: Early-Career Researcher Impact. 412 postdoctoral researchers cannot complete the publications required for independent faculty positions. Expected career progression: each postdoc, if completing their publications, would mentor 5-8 PhD students over a career. 156 early-career independent researchers lose grants before establishing research programmes — most cannot re-apply due to grant history requirements. Estimated career loss: 156-200 independent research careers that will not be established.
nodes.inspection_detail: Each lost career represents not just one researcher — it represents all the researchers they would have trained. The cascade multiplies through mentorship chains. One lost faculty position eliminates 5-8 subsequent researchers over a career.

nodes.label: PIPELINE_MULTIPLIER_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Research Pipeline Multiplier Calculation. Direct grant value: 1.8B. Add: sunk investment not recovered (1.1B). Add: publications not produced (estimated 12,400 papers over 5 years at avg 85K research value each = 1.05B). Add: trained researchers not produced (estimated 3,100 PhD-years at avg 120K value = 372M). Add: methodologies not transferred (estimated 200-400 novel methodologies, 25-50M each = 5-20B over 15 years). Total estimated 5-15 year impact: 8-22B. Multiplier: 4.4-12x the immediate fiscal saving.
nodes.inspection_detail: The 1.8B saving produces 8-22B in actual research output loss over 5-15 years. The multiplier (4.4-12x) exists because each grant represents compounded productive capacity — not just the grant cost but all that would have been built on top of it.

nodes.label: PEER_REVIEW_NETWORK_EFFECT
nodes.surface_text: Peer Review Network Disruption. When 847 researchers lose grants simultaneously: reviewing capacity drops — remaining researchers are absorbed managing career crises. Review times for surviving grants increase (estimated 40-60% increase). This delays publication of surviving research. Quality of review declines as reviewers are overloaded. The review network is a shared resource — mass terminations impose external costs on all remaining researchers.
nodes.inspection_detail: The network externality: terminated researchers impose costs on surviving researchers by reducing the shared peer review resource. This is a negative externality not captured in any individual grant analysis.

nodes.label: COMPARABLE_EVENT_ANALYSIS
nodes.surface_text: Comparable Funding Termination Events — Historical Data. 3 prior regional research funding terminations of similar scale: Event A (12 years ago, 620 grants): 5-year research output in affected fields declined 58% vs baseline, recovery to pre-termination output took 11 years. Event B (8 years ago, 380 grants): grant productivity (publications per M invested) took 9 years to return to pre-termination levels. Event C (5 years ago, 290 grants): 42% of affected early-career researchers left academic research permanently.
nodes.inspection_detail: Historical data across 3 comparable events: 8-11 year recovery timelines, 42-58% output decline during recovery, permanent career losses. The compounding multiplier effect is empirically confirmed by historical precedent.

nodes.label: ROOT_CAUSE_DETERMINATION
nodes.surface_text: Root Cause — Pipeline Compounding Multiplier Omitted from Fiscal Analysis. The 1.8B fiscal saving is real but represents only the avoided future disbursement. The full cost includes: 1.1B sunk investment that produces no return, 1-21B in research output not produced over 5-15 years, 156-200 research careers not established (each representing 5-8 downstream researchers not trained), and peer review network degradation affecting all surviving research. The total 5-15 year impact is estimated at 4.4-12x the stated saving. The fiscal analysis was correct within its scope — it did not consider pipeline compounding because that requires temporal extension beyond single-year accounting.
nodes.inspection_detail: Core concept: pipeline compounding cascade — when cumulative productive capacity is disrupted, the impact is not proportional to the immediate cost but to all value that would have been built on top of it. A grant is not just its disbursement value — it is the present value of all future output it enables. Terminating grants destroys compounded value at a ratio far exceeding the immediate saving.

optimal_path.verdict: Root cause: Fiscal analysis counted avoided disbursements (1.8B) but not pipeline compounding multiplier (4.4-12x). Each grant represents compounded productive capacity — sunk investment, publications, trained researchers, methodologies. Historical comparable events confirm 8-11 year recovery timelines and 4-12x multiplier. The analysis was correct within its scope; the scope did not include temporal compounding.
